category: UNESCO triple-designated tuff cone — climb to the rim

en_fields.description: **Seongsan Ilchulbong (성산일출봉, 'Sunrise Peak')** is a **5,000-year-old Surtseyan-type hydrovolcanic tuff cone** — a rare landform created when an underwater basalt eruption broke the sea surface, the magma met seawater, and the resulting steam-explosive ash built a **182 m fortress-shaped crater** rim instead of a typical lava cone. The result is what UNESCO formally calls **'an exceptional example of an emergent Surtseyan-type volcanic edifice'**. The crater is **600 m wide × 90 m deep**, ringed by **99 jagged rim rocks** that local lore likens to a stone crown. Seongsan carries the **Triple UNESCO designation**: **World Natural Heritage (inscribed 2007-07-02)** as part of the **'Jeju Volcanic Island and Lava Tubes' property**, **Biosphere Reserve (2002)**, and **Global Geopark (2010-10)** — a stack matched only by Hallasan itself among Korean sites. **18 Imperial Japanese WWII caves** (kamikaze suicide-boat shelters, 1945) are tunneled into the seaward base. Trail: **0.8 km one-way, ~140 m elevation gain, 60 min round trip** on wide stone steps. **Haenyeo (해녀) demonstrations** run **daily at 13:30 and 15:00** at the rocky cove below the cone — Jeju women divers (UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage 2016) descend without scuba gear. Admission updated **April 2025**: **Adult ₩5,000 / Youth+Child ₩2,500 / Group adult ₩1,600**, free under 7 and over 65. The summit deck is also part of the **Yeongjusipgyeong (Jeju 10 Scenic Wonders) canon**, and remains Korea's premier **annual New Year sunrise pilgrimage** destination — 50,000+ visitors ascend before dawn on January 1, with municipal sunrise-festival programming since 1995. **Udo Island (우도, 'Cow Island')** — Korea's largest secondary volcanic island — is clearly visible 4 km offshore from the summit, accessible by **15-min ferry from Seongsan Port** (₩10,500 round-trip) for guests with extra time on a different itinerary.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: **The day's UNESCO summit-anchor and east-route hero stop.** Three reasons Seongsan is the afternoon-1 anchor at 12:50. (1) **Triple-UNESCO weight**: Seongsan carries the only **simultaneous triple-designation** on the day's path — World Heritage (2007) + Global Geopark (2010) + Biosphere Reserve (2002). On a hydrangea-festival tour, this is the single biggest non-floral anchor and the stop that earns the tour its 'plus UNESCO' framing in catalog. (2) **Light + climb timing**: the 12:50–14:05 window post-lunch is optimal for the 25-min rim climb because guests are fresh-fueled but pre-mid-afternoon-heat (especially valuable in June hydrangea-season heat), and the early-afternoon south-southwest light flatters the tuff-cone's east-facing crater walls for photography. (3) **Pacing pivot for the floral finale**: Seongsan delivers the day's most physically engaged moment (climb), creating earned stillness for the gentler **Ilchul Land hydrangea preview** and **Hueree hydrangea finale** that follow — the route's deliberate geology-then-flowers narrative arc.

convenience.restroom: Available at entrance and base

en_fields.description: **Daepo Jusangjeolli (대포 주상절리, 'Jisatgae')** is a **1 km stretch of hexagonal columnar-jointed basalt cliff** on the Jungmun coast — designated **Natural Monument No. 443 on 2005-01-06**. The columns rise **30–40 m** from the sea, packed in tightly fitted **5- to 7-sided polygons** (mostly hexagons) — a geometry produced when **molten basaltic lava from a Hallasan flank eruption (~250,000–140,000 years ago) reached the ocean and contracted as it cooled**, fracturing perpendicular to the cooling surface. Globally, columnar jointing is best known from **Giant's Causeway (UK)**, **Devils Postpile (US)**, and **Fingal's Cave (Scotland)** — Jusangjeolli is **East Asia's premier example** and the most accessible. The 800 m **boardwalk loop** delivers visitors to a railed cliff edge directly above the columns; **on high-tide days waves break against the columns and throw spray up to 20 m**, occasionally over the rail. Admission: **Adult ₩2,000 / Youth ₩1,000 / FREE under 7 and over 65**. The site is part of the **Jeju Global Geopark (UNESCO designated 2010-10)** — one of **13 official geosites** on the island, alongside Seongsan Ilchulbong, Manjanggul Lava Tube, and Suwolbong. The clifftop deck is **barrier-free** (wheelchair/stroller accessible). Three viewing platforms along the loop give different column angles: the **first platform** shows the columns in profile (best for the hexagonal-geometry shot), the **mid-platform** looks straight down for top-of-column patterns, and the **far platform** captures the 1 km coastal sweep with Hallasan as backdrop on clear days. **Photographic peak window: 14:00–16:00**, when westering sunlight lights the column faces frontally; sunset visits bring silhouette compositions but lose column-detail texture. Hours: **09:00–18:00 (last entry 17:30)**, year-round operation.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: **Jusangjeolli Cliff (Natural Monument No. 443, designated 2005-01-06)** is the day's geological anchor — **East Asian peer to Giant's Causeway, Devils Postpile, and Fingal's Cave**: Hallasan-flank lava reaching the sea ~250,000–140,000 years ago contracted perpendicular to the cooling face, producing the iconic hexagonal-pentagonal columnar joints. **Winter visit advantage**: the **basalt cliff is dramatically backlit by low-angle winter afternoon sun** (sunset window ~17:30 in Dec–Jan), and **winter ocean swells** (Nov–Feb peak) deliver some of the year's most dramatic wave-crash scenes against the columnar cliff face — a markedly different atmosphere from the calmer summer surf. The **paved cliff-edge walkway** (~15-min full circuit, railings throughout) is **family-safe even in winter wind**; the operator advises layered jackets here as the cliff is wind-exposed. Pairing with Hallasan in the morning gives the day a **vertical geology arc** — alpine snow origin → coastal lava terminus, the same volcanic system from peak to sea. **Position sixth (16:00)** also positions the group for the day's golden-hour light over the basalt + ocean.

name_en: Jeju Cruise Terminal Pickup (Jeju Port — north)

en_fields.description: Your driver and English-speaking guide meet your party at the **Jeju International Passenger Terminal** (제주항 국제여객터미널, address: 191 Imhang-ro, Jeju-si — UN/LOCODE **KRCHA**), the northern coast cruise port that opened in **2015** to receive international cruise vessels. The terminal has two berths — the **Main Berth (360 m, 12 m water depth, 140,000-ton capacity)** and an **Emergency Berth (306 m, 11.5 m water depth, 85,000-ton capacity)** — handling **one cruise ship at a time**. Pickup is timed for **30 minutes after your ship's posted clearance**, absorbing immigration variance during multi-ship arrivals. The driver carries an English/Korean name placard, a printed itinerary card (with all stop times and Korean-script addresses for emergency taxi use), and the operator's 24/7 contact number. Vehicle is parked in the **100-bus parking capacity lot** adjacent to the arrival hall. Before boarding, the guide reviews the day's flow, all-aboard time, weather, and any port-day adjustments. If your guests need currency exchange (Jeju Bank), a SIM/Wi-Fi rental, or use of the multilingual tourist information center, the guide accompanies — these are all on-site within the terminal. Per **South Korea's Ministry of Culture, Sports & Tourism**, **810,000 cruise tourists arrived on 414 ships in 2024** — nearly tripling the prior year — so terminal congestion is realistic on multi-ship days.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: **Jeju Port (north) is the primary cruise gateway** for vessels under 140,000 GT — and the natural origin point for the east-route circuit (Seongsan / Seopjikoji / Seongeup), since all three core POIs sit within a 60–80 minute drive of this terminal. For a **bus tour with 25–40 pax**, the 30-minute post-clearance buffer is essential — large groups disembark slowly and immigration backups are common during multi-ship arrivals. The 100-bus parking capacity at Jeju Port also means the coach can be **right at the arrival hall door** (no shuttle wait), keeping the day's schedule tight. If your ship docks at Gangjeong (south) instead of here, the **southern variant of this tour** is the correct match — the drive from Gangjeong to Seongsan would consume 70+ minutes one-way, eating the day.

convenience.restroom: At meeting point (subway station / hotel lobby / port terminal)

smartNotes.tip: Arrive 5–10 minutes before scheduled pickup; bring photo ID for cruise/DMZ tours

visitBasics.walking: Minimal

category: Joseon-era folk heritage — UNESCO/National Folklore designation

en_fields.description: **Seongeup Folk Village (성읍 민속마을)** is a **living Joseon-era walled town founded in 1423** during the reign of **Sejong the Great** as Jeong-eui-hyeon (정의현), one of the three administrative seats of Joseon-era Jeju. The village served continuously as a **county administrative center until 1914** — nearly **500 years** — and retains **110 traditional structures** today within the original eupseong (읍성) walled-town footprint, designed on **Korean feng shui principles** typical of Joseon county capitals. The entire village is designated **Important Folklore Cultural Heritage of South Korea**, with **multiple individual buildings** carrying separate heritage status. At the village center stand **two ancient trees designated Natural Monuments of South Korea** — a **Saw-leaf Zelkova (느티나무)** and a **Japanese Hackberry (팽나무)**, both estimated **600+ years old** and revered as the village's spiritual anchor. Historical scars are visible: a **major fire in 1826 destroyed ~80 buildings**, and the village suffered fire damage **during the Jeju Uprising in 1948** — both events preserved as historical record on village signage. The **peak household count (historical, pre-modern) was ~1,500** — today the village remains inhabited by ~250 families, many actively maintaining traditional crafts. Drama filming credit: **'Dae Jang Geum (Jewel in the Palace)' (MBC, 2003–2004)**, the international K-drama hit. Local specialty: **Omegisul (오메기술)** — a **rice-based traditional Jeju liquor** brewed by **one family for multiple generations**, with the brewing demonstrated and tasted free of charge at the home-brewery within the village. **FREE admission**; outdoor walking dominates (~70% outdoor / 30% indoor for individual hanok visits).
en_fields.whyOnRoute: **Heritage anchor and the day's only inland Joseon stop.** Three east-route reasons Seongeup belongs at 10:30. (1) **Geographic pivot**: Seongeup sits at the inland Pyoseon-myeon junction where the Hamdeok→Seongsan eastbound coastal arc detours south through the central highlands. Locking it as the morning's second substantive stop converts what would otherwise be 50 min of throughput driving into a heritage anchor with no detour penalty. (2) **Pace-and-tone counterpoint to the floral afternoon**: the rest of the day (Ilchul Land, Hueree) is hydrangea-immersive and garden-coded; Seongeup's stone-walled hanok lanes + pony-led Joseon administrative history give the route its only **deeply-cultural, unhurried walking** stop, preventing the day from collapsing into a floral-only photography tour. (3) **UNESCO-adjacent layer**: National Folklore Cultural Heritage No. 188 designation pairs naturally with the afternoon's UNESCO-triple Seongsan, building the east-route's heritage-plus-volcanism through-line that distinguishes it from the Tour 2 (southwest) garden-plus-coast variant.

convenience.restroom: Public restrooms at village entrance

smartNotes.tip: Hire a free volunteer guide at the entrance for cultural context; respect that residents actually live here — don't enter homes uninvited

category: Volcanic Cape — Tadao Ando Architecture + Bangdupo Lighthouse + Drama Filming

en_fields.description: **Seopjikoji (섭지코지)** is a narrow volcanic cape on Jeju's east coast — the name compounds **'Seopji' (narrow piece of land) + 'Koji' (Jeju dialect for 'cape')**. The **2.1 km cliff-edge trail (~30 min, only 14 m elevation gain)** is one of the island's most photographed coastal walks, anchored by three cultural-architectural moments. **(1) Bulgeun Oreum (붉은 오름)** — a red-tinged volcanic parasitic cone at the cape's tip, capped by the **octagonal Bangdupo Lighthouse (방두포 등대)** which **flashes every 4 seconds** and remains in operation as a coastal navigation aid. **(2) The Hyeopja Yeondae Beacon Fire Station (협자연대)** — a **4 m tall × 9 m diameter Joseon-era smoke-signal mound built of volcanic stone**, designated **Special Monument No. 23-2** by Jeju Province — one of 38 such yeondae beacons that ringed the island as a 16th–18th-century early-warning system against pirate raids. **(3) Tadao Ando architecture** — Pulitzer-laureate Japanese architect **Tadao Ando (안도 다다오)** designed two buildings on site: the **Glass House (글라스 하우스)** restaurant and the **Yumin Art Gallery (유민 미술관)**, both characteristic of Ando's signature concrete-and-light minimalism, completed for the Phoenix Island resort development. Offshore, the **10 m volcanic neck Seonbawi (선바위, 'Seon Rock')** is featured in local **dragon king's youngest son** legend. Drama-filming credits include **'All In' (2003, SBS), 'My Girl' (2005, SBS), 'Legend of Gingko', 'The Uprising', and 'Cheonilya'**. **Spring canola fields** flood the slopes **March–April (yellow)**; **summer hydrangea blooms** follow in **June (blue/violet)**. **FREE admission** to the cape; Yumin Art Gallery and Glass House restaurant separately ticketed/reserved.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: **Seopjikoji's Tadao Ando architecture** is a small-group product highlight — the **Pulitzer-laureate concrete minimalism** of Glass House restaurant + Yumin Art Gallery rewards close-up viewing, and the small-group window allows guests to **enter Yumin Art Gallery** (~30 min self-paced) and order coffee at Glass House (overlooking the 1.3 km coastal cape) without coach-group queues. The **Joseon Hyeopja Yeondae Beacon** (Special Monument No. 23-2, 4 m × 9 m volcanic-stone smoke-signal mound) and the **Bangdupo Lighthouse** (octagonal, flashes every 4 sec) get individual narration. For the **5 K-drama filming credits** (All In, My Girl, Legend of Gingko, The Uprising, Cheonilya), the small-group product allows fan guests to find specific scene locations with the guide — a coach-group can't accommodate this kind of detour-style attention.

convenience.restroom: Available at parking lot and Aqua Planet area

smartNotes.photo: The lighthouse with cliff backdrop is iconic; canola fields with Seongsan Ilchulbong in distance is the spring signature shot
smartNotes.facilities: Aqua Planet Jeju aquarium and several cafés on site
smartNotes.tip: Combine with Seongsan Ilchulbong visit (10 min drive); spring (canola) and autumn (eulalia grass) are peak photo seasons

category: Amorepacific tea-culture flagship + green-tea fields finale

en_fields.description: **O'Sulloc Tea Museum (오설록 티 뮤지엄, '오설록' meaning 'meeting snow + green')** is the tea-culture flagship of **Amorepacific Corporation** — Korea's largest beauty company. Founder **Suh Sung-whan personally pioneered the project in 1979**, reclaiming volcanic ash farmland on Jeju's Seogwang plateau when conventional wisdom held tea couldn't be grown commercially in Korea. Today the **Seogwang Dawon plantation (서광다원)** is **Korea's largest green-tea field**, producing **~700 tonnes annually** across Amorepacific's three Jeju estates. The museum building **opened 2001** in the plantation's heart and was named to **designboom's Top 10 art museums** for its minimalist green-roofed pavilion-amid-tea-rows design. **FREE admission**, daily **09:00–18:00**, ~**2 million annual visitors**. The visit splits naturally into three segments: **(1) Museum interior** — tea production process, antique tea-ware collection from Tang dynasty (618–907 CE) onward, and rooftop observation deck with panoramic plantation view; **(2) Tea House cafe** — signature **green tea rolling cake (오설록 롤케이크)** and **green tea ice cream** served from the tea-leaf harvest year directly; **(3) Plantation walking paths** — gravel rows between tea bushes, popular for sunset photography at the western edge. **Innisfree Jeju House** (Amorepacific's natural cosmetics brand) is a **5-minute walk** for guests interested in the broader brand ecosystem — also free admission with optional tangerine soap DIY workshop (₩15,000). The museum building itself, designed by Korean architect **Mass Studies (Cho Min-suk)**, takes a deliberately low-profile horizontal form that mirrors the tea-row geometry rather than competing with the agricultural landscape — the architectural deference earning it both the designboom Top 10 listing and ongoing use as a case study in Korean contemporary museum design. Optional add-on: the **Osulloc Tea Class (₩30,000 per person, 60 min, advance booking required)** — guided tea-flight tasting with brewing technique demonstration on Korean ceramics, available daily **10:00–16:00**, English-speaking instruction on Wed/Sat sessions.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: **The day's tea-culture finale — and the southwest route's signature non-floral close.** Three reasons Osulloc closes the day at 16:45. (1) **Tea-color counterpoint to the day's hydrangea palette**: after 4 garden/cliff stops in varying flower-heavy palettes, Osulloc's **emerald-green tea-field rows + Innisfree Jeju House's white-glass architecture** give the day's photo set its closing color tone — saturated green that contrasts with the morning's hydrangea blues/pinks. (2) **Indoor-finale + restroom-buffer**: Osulloc's tea museum + cafe provide the day's **only indoor late-afternoon stop** — guests can use the museum's tea-tasting + ice-cream + restroom facilities to decompress before the **40-min westbound transit back to Jeju City**. The 60-min dwell is sized exactly to allow tea purchase + ice cream + 1 quick tea-field walk. (3) **Brand-cultural anchor**: Osulloc as the **Amorepacific tea flagship (founded 1979 by founder Suh Sung-whan)** ties the day's tour back to one of Korea's globally-recognized brands — a deliberate brand-cultural close that distinguishes this route from generic 'flower garden tours' and gives the day a clear cultural-economic finale story.

convenience.restroom: Multiple restrooms; aesthetically designed

en_fields.description: **Bulguksa Temple (불국사) — UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1995** (jointly with the nearby Seokguram Grotto) — is **the masterpiece of the golden age of Buddhist architecture in the Unified Silla dynasty (668-935 CE)** and is officially designated **Korea's Historic and Scenic Site No. 1**. The temple's name translates '**Temple of the Buddha Land**' and its layout intentionally maps the Buddhist cosmology onto stone terraces and wooden halls. Originally built in **528 CE under King Beopheung** as a small temple, the current grand layout was commissioned in **751 CE by Prime Minister Kim Daeseong** to honor his parents (the legend says he built Bulguksa for his earthly parents and the nearby Seokguram Grotto for his previous-life parents). The temple was completed in **774 CE** after Kim's death. The wooden halls were destroyed by fire during the **1592-1598 Imjin War**; rebuilding began in 1604 and continued through ~40 renovations until 1805. Major restoration was done **1969-1973 under President Park Chung-hee** to bring the temple to its current form. **The famous stone structures are original Silla** — preserved across 1,300+ years. Bulguksa houses **six Korean National Treasures**: (1) **Dabotap Pagoda (NT 20, 10.4 m)** — the ornate 'Many Treasures Pagoda' whose image appears on Korea's 10-won coin, (2) **Seokgatap Pagoda (NT 21, 8.2 m)** — the elegantly minimal 'Sakyamuni Pagoda,' inside which the **Pure Light Dharani Sutra was discovered in 1966 — dated 704-751 CE, this is the world's oldest extant woodblock-printed text, predating the Gutenberg Bible by 700+ years**, (3) **Yeonhwagyo + Chilbogyo bridges (NT 22)** — the Lotus Flower + Seven Treasures stone bridge stairway leading to Geuknakjeon (Hall of Pure Land), (4) **Cheongungyo + Baegungyo bridges (NT 23)** — the Blue Cloud + White Cloud bridge stairway leading to Jahamun Gate (the 34 steps = the 34 steps to enlightenment in Buddhist doctrine), (5) **Gilt-bronze seated Vairocana Buddha (NT 26)** in Birojeon, and (6) **Gilt-bronze seated Maitreya Bodhisattva (NT 27)** in Geuknakjeon. The visit follows the temple's pilgrimage axis: **Iljumun gate → Cheongungyo/Baegungyo bridges (admire from below; foot traffic banned) → Jahamun Gate → Daeungjeon Hall with Dabotap and Seokgatap in the courtyard → Birojeon Vairocana Hall → Geuknakjeon Pure Land Hall**. **Admission has been FREE since 2023** under the revised Cultural Heritage Protection Act. **Hours daily 09:00-18:00 (last admission 17:00)**. **Modest dress + remove shoes when entering halls** + **no drone photography**.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: The route's **single most significant cultural anchor** — Bulguksa is what comes to mind for most international travelers when they hear 'Gyeongju.' Pairing it as the afternoon's primary stop (vs. trying to also fit Seokguram Grotto, which is 20 min uphill and adds 90+ min) keeps the day at a reasonable 9-10-hour total. The 14:15 arrival is also after Bulguksa's busiest morning tour-bus arrivals (typically 10:00-12:00) and gives ~90 min of exploration during golden-hour light on the stone pagodas, the temple's most photogenic time.

convenience.restroom: Free public restrooms (designed in mini-temple style) inside the complex

smartNotes.tip: Modest dress; remove shoes when entering the halls; no drones; do not photograph monks or worshippers (signs marked clearly); the temple is still an active Jogye Order head temple (11th district)

category: Three-Tier Waterfall + Forest — 'Pond of the Emperor of Heaven'

en_fields.description: **Cheonjeyeon Falls (천제연, '연 = pond + 천제 = Emperor of Heaven')** is a **three-tier waterfall** in Jungmun's volcanic gorge, designated **Natural Monument No. 378 (1993)** for its rare ecological combination. The name comes from a **Joseon-era Daoist legend that seven nymphs (선녀, *seonnyeo*) descended from heaven nightly to bathe in the upper pond** — a myth reinforced by the dramatic site's microclimate of perpetual mist. The **1st tier (22 m fall, 21 m deep pond)** drops into a circular basalt cauldron and only flows after heavy rain — most days the upper pond sits glassy and reflective. The **2nd tier (30 m fall) flows year-round** — the dominant photographic subject. The 3rd tier completes the descent to the sea. The **120 m / 78 m-high Seonimgyo Bridge (선임교, 'Bridge of the Seven Nymphs')** crosses the gorge midway through the loop — its cast-bronze relief panels along both sides depict **all seven nymphs in flight**, a literal architectural realization of the founding legend. Surrounding forest is a **rare-plant habitat** including **Korean toadlily (***Tricyrtis macropoda***)** and the endangered **Mankyua chejuense fern** (Jeju endemic, IUCN Critically Endangered). Loop length: **0.8 km, ~30 min walk** with several steep stair sections. Admission: **Adult ₩2,500 / Youth ₩1,350 / FREE under 7 + over 65**. Cheonjeyeon is also a member of the **'Yeongjusipgyeong (영주십경, 10 Scenic Wonders of Jeju)'** Joseon-era literati canon — alongside Hallasan summit, Seongsan Sunrise, and Jeongbang Falls — meaning today's southwest loop covers **2 of the 10 (Hallasan + Cheonjeyeon)** in a single day. The biennial **Jeju Chilseonyeo (Seven Nymphs) Festival** held at the bridge in May (every odd year) reenacts the descent legend with traditional dance and musical performance — reservations required when the festival coincides with a tour date. Hours **09:00–18:00** year-round (last entry 17:30).
en_fields.whyOnRoute: **Cheonjeyeon Falls** ('Pond of the Heavenly Emperor', **Natural Monument No. 378**) is the day's three-tier waterfall closer — **NOT to be confused with Cheonjiyeon (천지연, single-tier, NM 379)** in downtown Seogwipo. **Winter advantage**: the **Seonimgyo Bridge** (선임교 — 'Bridge of Seven Nymphs' with seven-nymph carved relief, designed in 1984) is **illuminated after dark from ~17:30 in winter**, and the **late-afternoon visit (16:50–17:35)** captures the natural transition from daylight to the bridge's evening illumination — a **catalog-unique 'winter twilight' photography window** that summer tours miss entirely. **Three-tier cascade** structure: Tier 1 (22 m drop into emerald pool — water-only on rainy days; usually pool-only in winter dry season), Tier 2 (~30 m), Tier 3 (~5 m into final pool meeting the sea). **Yongyeon Pond** at Tier 1 is the protected habitat of the **Mutae eel** (marbled eel, NM 27 — same species as Cheonjiyeon's). **~10 min drive from Jusangjeolli** continues the cliff-to-falls geological flow. **Final substantive stop** — the day closes with the most delicate scenery before evening drop-off.

convenience.restroom: At entrance/parking area only — none past the ticket office

smartNotes.tip: The first tier is dry except after heavy rain — don't be surprised if it's not flowing; allow 1.5–2 hours to see all three tiers; do NOT confuse with Cheonjiyeon Falls (different falls in Seogwipo)

name_en: Micheon Cave at Ilchul Land (Manjanggul alternate)
category: Lava Tube + Theme Park — Manjanggul Alternate (UNESCO-property adjacent)

en_fields.description: **Ilchul Land (일출랜드)** is a **400,000 m² nature theme park** in Seongsan, opened **2002**, built around the **Micheon Cave (미천굴)** lava tube — a **1,700 m total length cave** with a **365 m artificially leveled walk-through section** open to visitors (~25-minute traverse). The cave is part of Jeju's **lava-tube system that shares geological lineage with Manjanggul Cave** — the UNESCO World Natural Heritage lava tube — and **functions as the operational alternate when Manjanggul closes for conservation maintenance or weather (typhoons, freezing)**. Where Manjanggul preserves its uneven natural floor as an authentic geological experience, Micheon's leveled walkway makes the cave **accessible to elderly visitors, families with strollers, and guests with mobility limitations** — a deliberate engineering choice that broadens the lava-tube experience to audiences who couldn't navigate Manjanggul. The interior is lit with **decorative seven-color lighting (gold, silver, bronze, red, black, blue, white)** highlighting cave formations — controversial among purists but practical for non-specialist tourist photography. Outside the cave, the park extends across **Subyeon Park, a reconstructed traditional Jeju village, Wild Flower Bonsai Garden, Cactus Greenhouse, Sculpture Street, and a Footbath Cafe (aroma foot spa)** — making this the **only Jeju attraction with an on-site Muslim prayer room** (a deliberate accessibility investment). Drama filming credit: **'Running Man' (런닝맨, SBS) Episode 89 (Alice-in-Wonderland themed, 2012)**. Admission: **Adult ₩12,000 / Youth ₩9,000 / Child ₩7,000**. Indoor/outdoor split is **30% indoor (cave + greenhouse)** / **70% outdoor (park grounds)** — meaningful for weather-flexibility planning.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: **Pre-lunch lava-tube anchor** — explicitly framed as the **UNESCO-property adjacent** chapter of the day's UNESCO theme. Where the nature-core sister tour uses Ilchulland as a mid-afternoon weather-flexible stop, this UNESCO-spots route positions it earlier in the day to keep the **'UNESCO + heritage' narrative arc tight**: Hamdeok (Olle Trail 19, semi-UNESCO-status walking heritage) → Seongeup (Important Folklore Cultural Heritage) → **Ilchulland Micheon Cave (Manjanggul lava-tube system — same UNESCO property as Manjanggul itself)** → afternoon Seongsan (Triple UNESCO) → finale Haenyeo Museum (UNESCO Intangible 2016). The pre-lunch placement also ensures cave temperature comfort (caves are 13–15°C year-round — pleasantly cool in summer, mildly cool in winter) before the day's warmest outdoor stretches.

convenience.restroom: Available at Ilchul Land park entrance

name_en: Lunch (East Jeju — local seafood or black pork)

en_fields.description: **East Jeju lunch stop** at a guide-vetted local restaurant in the Seongsan / Seongeup / Pyoseon area, chosen to fit cruise day-trip timing. **Two typical options**, selected based on group preference and availability: **(1) Jeju Black Pork Heukdwaeji BBQ** (제주 흑돼지) — Jeju's signature ingredient, the indigenous Black Pig was designated **Natural Monument of Korea** in 2015, and pork-belly cuts are grilled over charcoal at the table with traditional banchan; or **(2) Galchi Jorim** (갈치조림 — braised hairtail with radish, soy-chili sauce) — eastern Jeju's famous seafood dish, drawing on the daily catch from Seongsan and Seongeup-area harbors. Vegetarian options are available at most venues with advance notice (the guide confirms with the restaurant ~30 min ahead). Lunch duration is **~60 minutes**, fitting the cruise day-trip's tight timeline (full day usually 8 hours port-to-port). Beverages: barley tea (free), local **Jeju Beer** (~₩6,000), or **Hallasan Soju** (Jeju's signature distilled spirit, ~₩5,000). All restaurants in the rotation accept **major cards (Visa/Mastercard) + cash KRW**; bilingual photo menus (English/Chinese) are standard at the cruise-tour-vetted venues. Lunch is **NOT included** in the standard cruise tour pricing — typical out-of-pocket is **₩15,000–25,000 per person** (varies by dish); inclusive lunch upgrade available on booking.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: **Small-group lunch experience** — 75-minute window (vs 60 min on the bus product) at a guide-vetted East Jeju restaurant, allowing more deliberate ordering and conversation. The guide stays with the group throughout the meal (vs typical coach-tour practice of guide-eats-separately), facilitating menu translation, recommending dishes, and answering Jeju-cuisine questions in real time. **Black Pork BBQ** (Natural Monument 2015 indigenous Black Pig) at-table charcoal preparation is the signature option; vegetarian guests get a confirmed Bibimbap + Mom-guk plate with prior notice. The longer lunch window also serves as **schedule slack** — if morning UNESCO sites ran long for photography, the team can absorb up to 15 minutes here without compressing the afternoon.

en_fields.description: Your driver and English-speaking guide meet your party at the **Gangjeong Port Cruise Terminal** (강정 크루즈항 / 서귀포 크루즈터미널 — UN/LOCODE **KRSPO**), officially the **'Jeju Civilian-Military Complex Port for Beautiful Tourism'** (제주 민군복합형관광미항), located in Gangjeong Village on Seogwipo's southern coast. The terminal opened in **February 2016**; cruise passenger operations began **2017**. Unlike the smaller northern Jeju Port, Gangjeong has **TWO cruise berths** — the **Western Seawall (Berths 11–12, 420 m, 12–15 m depth, 220,000-ton capacity)** and the **Southern Seawall (Berths 21–22, 690 m, 17 m depth, 220,000-ton capacity)** — handling **two large cruise ships simultaneously**. Pickup is timed for **30 minutes after your ship's posted clearance**, with the driver carrying an English/Korean placard. The vehicle is parked in the **53-bus outdoor parking area** (which fills quickly when both berths are occupied — guides arrive early on dual-ship days). Beginning **May 1, 2025**, **Adora Magic City began semi-homeporting at Gangjeong** with 33 roundtrip voyages planned through end-2025 (5–6 day Shanghai ↔ Jeju ↔ Japan circuits, 60–120 Korean passengers per voyage), increasing the port's profile for both Korean and international cruise lines. The terminal has **better wheelchair accessibility than Jeju Port (north)** per VisitKorea — flat interior, moving walkways, elevators throughout. If guests dock at Gangjeong, the **southern itinerary** is mandatory: the drive north to Seongsan Ilchulbong is **70+ min one-way**, eating the day.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: **Small-group pickup at Gangjeong** — the dual-berth 220,000-ton port handles larger cruise ships (Adora Magic City semi-homeport since May 1, 2025; Costa, MSC, Royal Caribbean), and the small-group product's **8–15 pax minicoach** has zero parking constraint at the 53-bus lot, even on dual-ship days when the lot fills with coach-tour vehicles. The personalized welcome includes confirming guest preferences for the day (Hallasan 1100 wetland duration, Cheonjiyeon trail pace, Olle Market food priorities). Gangjeong's **better wheelchair accessibility** vs Jeju Port (north) makes this the preferred south route for mobility-considerate small groups.

en_fields.description: Pickup is consolidated at three central Busan subway stations to make the early morning departure manageable: **Busan Station (Line 1)** ~08:00 for guests near Choryang/Nampo-dong, **Seomyeon Station (Lines 1 & 2)** ~08:15 for mid-city hotels around Lotte Hotel and Seomyeon Medical Street, and **Haeundae Station (Line 2)** ~08:30 for guests in the eastern beachfront resort district. From the final pickup at Haeundae the coach takes the **Gyeongbu Expressway / Donghae Expressway** northeast to Gyeongju — total transit ~75-90 minutes (~85 km / ~50 mi) depending on traffic. The early start is essential during cherry blossom peak (last week of March / first week of April) because Bomun Lake's parking lots can fill from 09:30 onward and the Bulguksa cherry-blossom approach road becomes very congested by 10:00. The driver will share a printed Korean-English itinerary card at pickup; please be at your stop 5 minutes early. Light snacks (banana, water) are provided for the coach ride; lunch is at the guests' own expense in Gyeongju (~₩10,000-15,000).
en_fields.whyOnRoute: Three pickup points cover Busan's three main hotel zones without forcing guests to transit at dawn. Sequencing northwest→east minimizes coach distance and lands the group at Bomun Lake by ~10:00 — early enough to park comfortably during cherry blossom peak when the Bomun area becomes the busiest tourist site in southeast Korea.

en_fields.description: **Drop-off reverses the morning pickup arc** along Route 1132 westbound, with four drop points across Jeju City: **Dongmun Traditional Market (동문시장)** as the first drop near Tapdong/Sarabong waterfront (handy for guests who want to extend into Dongmun's evening street-food scene), then **Jeju City Hall (제주시청)** for the Yeon-dong / Nohyeong / Old Town hotel cluster, then **Jeju International Airport (제주공항)** for guests with same-day departures or airport-area hotels, then **Hagwi-dong (하귀동)** as the westernmost drop. **Standard arrival window: ~17:50 Dongmun, ~18:05 City Hall, ~18:15 Airport, ~18:25 Hagwi.** Guests catching late evening flights are advised to choose the **Airport drop** and pad at least **2.5 hours** of buffer for international-departure check-in given the day's potential +30 min weather-cushion variance. The guide collects feedback cards during transit and confirms each drop point with each guest before disembarking.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: **Operational close to the east-route loop.** The reverse-of-pickup ordering means the coach naturally encounters Dongmun first as it re-enters the city from the east, then arcs westward through City Hall + Airport + Hagwi — there is no backtracking. Drop-off is also where the guide collects guest feedback cards while everyone is still in transit and the day is fresh.

Total transit ~75-90 minutes depending on Friday/weekend traffic. Drop-off sequence reverses the morning pickup: **Haeundae Station ~17:45 → Seomyeon ~18:00 → Busan Station ~18:15**. Guests staying near other locations can request a closer drop-off if it's along the coach route; please mention preferences to the guide before departure. The coach is equipped with onboard Wi-Fi (signal-dependent) and USB charging ports — good time to back up the day's photos. During cherry blossom peak weekends (typically last weekend of March + first weekend of April) the Gyeongbu Expressway can have moderate congestion between Yangsan and Gyeongju Junction; the driver may opt for the parallel Donghae Expressway depending on real-time traffic data.

Returning before 19:00 also matches the typical Busan tour-coach return contract window.

en_fields.description: **Suwon Hwaseong (수원화성) — UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1997** — Korea's only walled fortress city preserved with its original 10-volume construction record (Hwaseong Seongyeok Uigwe, separately inscribed on the **UNESCO Memory of the World Register in 2007**). Built **1794-1796 in just 2 years 9 months** under King Jeongjo (정조, 22nd Joseon king, r. 1776-1800) — the king's project to relocate the Joseon capital from Hanyang (Seoul) to Suwon and to honor his executed father Crown Prince Sado, who was sealed alive in a rice chest by his own father King Yeongjo in 1762. Designed by **Jeong Yak-yong (정약용, also known as Dasan, 1762-1836)** — Joseon's leading scholar-engineer of the Silhak (practical learning) school — Hwaseong integrated traditional Korean defensive architecture with Chinese siege defense theory and emerging Western fortification science. Jeong Yak-yong invented the **geojunggi (거중기) pulley-and-block crane** based on Western mechanical principles studied through Chinese sources — the first documented use of Western-style modern engineering in Korean construction. **5.74 km wall circumference, 41 strategic structures**: 4 main gates (**Janganmun north** — the LARGEST EXISTING ROYAL GATE IN KOREA, larger than Seoul's Sungnyemun; **Paldalmun south** — the only gate at sea level; **Hwaseomun west** — National Treasure No. 403; **Changnyongmun east** — reconstructed 1975), 4 angle towers, 4 command posts (jangdae), 5 sentry platforms, 5 gun batteries, archery platforms, sluice gates, and a beacon tower. Wall height varies 4-6 meters, thickness 5 meters at base. **Severely damaged during Korean War (1950-1953); rebuilding began 1975** using the Uigwe construction record — original sections coexist with reconstructed portions. The 120-min stop allows the **most comprehensive walking experience of any tour in the trio** — the standard Janganmun → Hwaseomun (1.2 km, ~30 min) plus the climb up to **Seojangdae (West Command Post) atop Paldalsan Mountain** for the highest viewpoint and the king's strategic command position; or alternatively, the descent past **Hwahongmun (north sluice gate)** with its 7 arches over Suwoncheon Stream — the most photographed structure of the fortress, especially in spring (cherry blossoms) and autumn (foliage). **FREE for hanbok-wearers + last Wed of month + seniors 65+ + children ≤6.**
en_fields.whyOnRoute: Tour 3's heritage anchor — given the compact 3-stop design, the fortress carries the day's full UNESCO + Joseon-era narrative weight. The 120-min budget (vs. Tour 1's 75 min and Tour 2's 60 min) allows guests to walk a longer section AND climb to Seojangdae for the panoramic viewpoint OR detour to Hwahongmun for the photogenic 7-arch sluice gate. Tour 3 SPECIFICALLY excludes Hwaseong Haenggung (which Tours 1 and 2 cover) — keeping the day to 3 substantive stops. As the morning anchor of a 3-stop pivot day-trip (Hwaseong → Starfield → Waujeongsa), Hwaseong delivers UNESCO World Heritage credentials in a **focused 90-120 min walking loop** centered on the Janganmun (largest existing royal gate in Korea — larger than Seoul's Sungnyemun), Hwahongmun water-gate, and Seojangdae western command post. The fortress's Korean-War damage and 1975-onwards reconstruction (using the 1801 Uigwe construction record, UNESCO Memory of the World since 2007) parallels the broader reconstruction-of-divided-Korea narrative that the Waujeongsa stop revisits in Buddhist-religious form.

convenience.restroom: At Janganmun, Hwaseomun, Paldalmun, Yeonmudae
convenience.parking: Coach drop-off at Janganmun lot; designated coach parking at Yeonmudae

smartNotes.photo: **Hwahongmun (north sluice gate)** with 7 arches over Suwoncheon Stream — the iconic shot, especially in spring (cherry blossoms) and autumn (foliage). **Janganmun (largest existing royal gate in Korea)** at golden hour. **Seojangdae (west command post)** sunset over Paldalsan Mountain — the panoramic king's-eye view.

The **120-min budget allows two photo focal points**: choose Seojangdae (highest viewpoint) OR Hwahongmun (most photographed structure) per group preference.

en_fields.description: **Starfield Library Suwon (별마당 도서관 수원)** — inside **Starfield Suwon, Shinsegae's flagship 'second-generation' Starfield mall opened January 26, 2024** as the fifth Starfield mall and the largest by gross floor area outside Hanam (~334,000 m²). Designed in collaboration with **BCHO Partners (Cho Byoung-soo)** — the Korean firm also responsible for the original Starfield Library at COEX (2017) — Suwon's library represents the second-generation iteration with **bookshelves rising up to 22 meters tall**, among the tallest cylindrical bookshelf structures in any Korean public space. The library spans **4 floors** (1F-4F atrium void) housing approximately **70,000 books and magazines** including Korean and international publications, periodicals, art books, and a dedicated children's literature section on 4F. **FREE and open to the public** — no library card, no admission fee, accessible during all mall hours (10:00-22:00 daily). Books cannot be borrowed (read-on-site only). Distinctive features: **Vinyl Record Listening Lounge** with curated Korean indie + international jazz/classic rock vinyl; **monthly rotating art exhibition** in the atrium space partnering with Korean contemporary artists; and **seasonal cherry blossom installation in the central atrium (March-April)** with thousands of pink LED petals — one of Suwon's most photographed spring spots. Adjacent mall amenities include: **Eatopia food hall (B1F)** with 30+ restaurants from quick-service Korean (5,000-10,000 KRW) to premium dining (40,000+ KRW); **Aquafield Suwon water park + Korean spa (B1-2F)** separate paid admission ~25,000-35,000 KRW; **Toy Kingdom (4F, Korea's largest toy store)**; and a CGV/MEGABOX dual cinema setup. **Free Starfield shuttle bus** runs from Suwon Station Exit 4 every 10-15 min (~10 min ride) — the easiest access from public transit. The 'modern Korea' counterweight after three Joseon-heritage stops, providing covered indoor finish that buffers any late-afternoon weather risk.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: The 'modern Korea' counterweight after three heritage stops (fortress + palace + folk village). Shinsegae's flagship 2nd-generation Starfield mall opened January 26, 2024; the four-floor library with 22 m bookshelves and vinyl-listening section is the architectural highlight. **All-indoor stop** — buffers any late-afternoon weather risk and provides genuine multi-generational appeal (kids → Toy Kingdom; teens → vinyl + bookshelves; adults → café + shopping). Starfield Library is the **single most Instagram-photographed indoor space in Greater Seoul outside of central Seoul** (per Korean travel-media surveys 2024-25) — the 22m bookshelves (vs Seoul COEX's 13m) span 4 mall floors (4F-7F atrium void), and the rooftop-level **'The Planets' installation by Canadian novelist-designer Douglas Coupland** is exclusive to the Suwon location. **FREE admission, no membership, photos permitted** — making this a no-friction closing photo stop after the more-paid Hwaseong/Haenggung/Folk-Village earlier in the day. From Suwon Station: Subway Line 1 to Hwaseo Station Exit 1/4 (~3 min on subway, 8-min walk), or free Starfield shuttle bus (every 10-15 min from Suwon Station Exit 4).

convenience.restroom: Multiple restrooms on every floor; family-friendly with diaper-changing stations
convenience.parking: Underground parking ~5,000 spaces; first 3 hours FREE with mall purchase 30,000 KRW+; coach lot for groups

Avoid Saturday afternoons (peak crowd density). 1-2 monthly closure days — verify online before visit.

category: Hydrangea tunnel + 172,000 m² largest camellia arboretum in the East

en_fields.description: **Camellia Hill (카멜리아힐, Jeju Camellia Arboretum)** is a **172,000 m² botanical garden** in Andeok, **the largest camellia arboretum in the East** by acreage and species count: **6,000 camellia trees representing over 500 species sourced from 80 countries**, accumulated over **30+ years** of curation by founder Yang Jong-mun. The garden's signature is its **year-round flowering calendar**, deliberately engineered so that visiting in any season produces a different color palette: **November–March = camellia peak** (red, pink, white, cream-beige varieties — the garden's namesake season); **March–May = cherry blossoms + tulips + late camellias** layered together in dense color mass; **June–August = hydrangeas** (both outdoor stands and a climate-controlled greenhouse showing year-round display); **September–October = fall foliage + late-season bloomers**. Walking paths thread through themed sections — **European Garden, Korean Native Forest Section, Cherry Blossom Walk, Hydrangea Field** — connected by a winding 2.5-km loop that takes ~60–75 min to complete at a leisurely pace. Notable architectural moment: a **glass greenhouse + gallery + cafe** complex at the entrance, which serves as both indoor weather refuge (the only ~15% indoor portion of the visit) and a tea/coffee dwell space with garden views. **Drama filming credit**: variety show **'EXchange / Transit Love 3' (환승연애)** featured Camellia Hill as the location of Ju-won and Yu-jeong's memorable date scene — a draw for fans of the K-reality genre. **Open 365 days a year**; **no outdoor lighting** means the garden closes at sundown (winter ~17:00, summer ~19:00). Admission: **Adult ₩12,000 / Youth + Senior ₩10,000 / Child (4–11) ₩9,000 / FREE under 3**.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: **The afternoon's hydrangea-tunnel anchor — and the day's photographic centerpiece.** Three southwest-route reasons Camellia Hill is the afternoon-2 anchor at 15:15. (1) **Hydrangea Tunnel — Korea's earliest blooming hydrangeas**: Camellia Hill houses **Korea's earliest-blooming hydrangeas (early April indoor + outdoor full bloom from June)**, with the **Hydrangea Hall pergola tunnel** as the day's definitive Instagram-anchor photo (giant blue/pink/purple hydrangea balls hanging above a covered walkway). The June 10–25 peak window aligns exactly with this tour's seasonal positioning. (2) **Garden-pacing complementarity**: Camellia Hill's **1.2 km / 0.75-mile walking loop** is gentler than Hallim's 9-themed-garden complexity, making it the day's **photo-immersive low-effort garden** after the morning's higher-density Hallim anchor. The two gardens together build a **garden-pacing arc** (intensive Hallim → relaxed Camellia Hill) that wouldn't work if reversed. (3) **Late-afternoon light**: the 15:15–16:30 window catches the **warm late-afternoon golden light** that flatters the hydrangea pinks and blues (midday flat light desaturates them, same logic as Hueree on the east route). Camellia Hill's signature photographers'-mirror installation also captures golden-hour reflections from this slot. **Sister-route note**: this stop matches the afternoon timing of Hueree on the east route — both sister tours close their hydrangea anchor in late afternoon for matching sun angles.

convenience.restroom: Available at entrance and within garden

smartNotes.tip: Visit before 10am or after 4pm in peak season to avoid crowds; pair with Osulloc (10 min drive)

en_fields.description: **Gamcheon Culture Village (감천문화마을)** — nicknamed both **'Korea's Machu Picchu'** and **'Korea's Santorini'** — is a hillside neighborhood in Saha-gu, west Busan, whose pastel-painted houses cascade down in stepped rows over the slope. The settlement began in the **1920s-1930s** when the Busan administration relocated working-class residents away from the port. The village's character was set in **1955** when **~800 families (about 4,000 people) of the Taegeukdo religious community** — a uniquely Korean syncretic religion blending Buddhist, Confucian, and yin-yang elements, founded by **Jo Cheol-je** during the Japanese colonial period — settled here as Korean War refugees fleeing the North. The Taegeukdo philosophy of '**allowing others to prosper**' directly shaped the village's most distinctive feature: houses are intentionally tiered so that **no roof blocks the view of the house behind it**, with the entire community visible to the sky and the sea. Gamcheon remained one of Busan's poorest neighborhoods until **2009**, when the Korean Ministry of Culture launched the **'Dreaming of Machu Picchu in Busan'** public-art revitalization project, followed by the **'Miro Miro' (maze) project in 2010** — adding 22+ commissioned murals, sculptures, and art installations including the iconic **'Little Prince and Fox' bench** at the Sky Garden viewpoint. Gamcheon won the **2012 UN-HABITAT Asian Townscape Award** and now welcomes **~1.4 million visitors annually**. The visit follows the village's signature stamp-map walking route: from the entrance at the **Tourist Information Center**, up to **Sky Garden viewpoint** for the panoramic rainbow-roof shot, then down through the labyrinthine alleys past murals, hanbok rental shops, craft workshops, and small cafes. **The village is a living residential community** — quiet voices required, no drones, no flash photography into homes, and rubbish carried out. Hours **09:00-18:00 Mar-Oct / 09:00-17:00 Nov-Feb (info center)**; **village itself open 24h, FREE entry**.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: The route's **most colorful and unique** stop — there is no other neighborhood like this anywhere in Korea. The Korean War refugee narrative directly threads from UN Memorial Cemetery (the moral memorial) → Gamcheon (the lived consequence: refugee settlement that became a community) → Jagalchi (the economic consequence: ajumma fish-vendors supporting their families). The 15:30 arrival lands ahead of the 18:00 info-center close while still giving guests ~1 hour of golden-hour light over the rainbow rooftops, the village's most photogenic time.

convenience.restroom: Public restrooms at the Tourist Information Center and around the main viewpoints

smartNotes.photo: Little Prince and Fox bench (long queue) and Haneul Maru / Sky Garden Observatory for the sweeping rainbow-roof and bay view
smartNotes.facilities: Hanbok rental, craft workshops, Korean candy stalls, cafes; small village museum at the entrance; the stamp map is the easiest way to navigate
smartNotes.tip: Residents live here — keep voices down, no drones, take rubbish with you; wear grippy shoes for the steep lanes; afternoon golden hour ~16:00-17:00 is the village's best light

visitBasics.hours: Daily 09:00–18:00 (Mar–Oct) / 09:00–17:00 (Nov–Feb); some shops/cafes keep their own hours
visitBasics.admission: Free village entry; optional Stamp Map ≈ 2,000 KRW
visitBasics.walking: Strenuous — very steep alleys and stairs; not stroller- or wheelchair-friendly in many sections

en_fields.description: **Jagalchi Market (자갈치시장) — Korea's largest seafood market** — anchors **Nampo-dong**, Busan's downtown shopping-and-dining district. The name 'Jagalchi' derives from **'jagal' (gravel)**, a reference to the pebbled shoreline where the market originated. After the 1876 opening of Busan to international trade and the post-Korean-War boom, **'Jagalchi Ajumma' women vendors** (war widows and refugees) built the market's identity as a tough, women-led seafood economy. The current **seagull-shaped modern building** (designed by Hyunjun Mihn) **reopened 2006** and houses **~400 vendors across 66,000 m²** — ground floor for live tanks (octopus, abalone, sea cucumber, halibut), second floor for raw-fish (hoe) restaurants where you can buy fish downstairs and have it filleted upstairs (chogochujang dipping sauce + soju standard). Market hours **05:00–22:00 daily**; **closed 1st and 3rd Tuesdays of each month** (verify before visiting). The annual **Jagalchi Cultural Tourism Festival** in October is the single biggest event. Surrounding **Nampo-dong** offers the **BIFF Square** (Busan International Film Festival origin, with handprints of Korean stars in the pavement — Park Chan-wook, Bong Joon-ho, etc.), **Gukje Market** (Korean War refugee market, still 1,500+ stalls), and **Gwangbok-ro Fashion Street**. Address: **52 Jagalchihaean-ro, Jung-gu**. Photo etiquette: **always ask before photographing live tanks or vendors**; Jagalchi Ajumma can be famously direct about cameras. Adjacent live-fish auctions begin at **04:00 daily** at the seafood traders' wholesale market that supplies the public market upstairs (advance booking required for tourist viewing of auctions).
en_fields.whyOnRoute: Jagalchi is **the most authentic 'Busan flavor' on a single-day tour** and the small-group's afternoon free-time anchor. 60 minutes (15 more than charter version) accounts for fixed-group reassembly — pairs Jagalchi's live-tank ground floor with Nampo-dong / BIFF Square / Gukje Market street walking. Optional fish cake (eomuk) snack stop that small-group budgets handle better than charter's premium positioning. For cruise guests this is the most authentic 'Busan flavor' photo opportunity in 45 minutes — live tanks + ajumma vendor culture + seagull-roof architecture in a walk-through that ends close to the ship's reboarding zone.

convenience.restroom: Public restrooms at BIFF Square and inside Jagalchi
convenience.parking: Use Lotte Department Store or BIFF Square public lots; market itself has no on-site parking

smartNotes.photo: **Seagull-shaped modern building (reopened 2006, architect Hyunjun Mihn)**, **live tanks on the ground floor**, **BIFF Square hand prints** (Park Chan-wook, Bong Joon-ho, etc.), and **Gukje Market** (1,500+ stalls). **Always ask before photographing vendors** — Jagalchi Ajumma can be famously direct about cameras.
smartNotes.tip: **Buy-downstairs / eat-upstairs hoe flow**: pick fresh seafood on the ground floor, have it filleted at a 2nd-floor restaurant (chogochujang + soju standard). **Sannakji (live cut octopus)** is Busan's culinary signature — the dish from *Old Boy* (2003).

en_fields.description: **United Nations Memorial Cemetery in Korea (UNMCK) — the ONLY UN memorial cemetery in the world** — established by **United Nations Command on January 18, 1951** during the Korean War, dedicated **April 6, 1951 by General Matthew Ridgway**, and **donated by the Republic of Korea to the UN in 1959** under a UN General Assembly resolution. The cemetery honors fallen personnel from the **22 sending nations** of the UN Command (16 combat + 6 medical) and today maintains **2,300 graves from 11 countries** (most repatriations occurred in the late 1950s). The 14-hectare grounds include the **Main Gate** designed by Korean modernist architect **Kim Joong-up (1966)**, the **Memorial Service Hall** with a 12-minute documentary in English, the **Symbolic Area** where the 22 sending-state flags fly, the **US Korean War Memorial** sculpture by Frank Gaylord (American Battle Monuments Commission, 2013), and the **Wall of Remembrance** dedicated in 2006 — **40,896 names** etched on **140 marble panels** representing every UN combatant killed in Korea. Each November 11 at 11:00 AM, the **'Turn Toward Busan' ceremony** is held simultaneously at the cemetery and at memorials in 22 nations worldwide — the only time of year all sending-state flags lower in unison. Admission is **free**, hours **09:00–18:00 (May–Sep) / 09:00–17:00 (Oct–Apr)**, and the entire grounds are **wheelchair-accessible** with paved paths. The cemetery is **administered by the international UN Memorial Cemetery Commission** and protected under **UN General Assembly Resolution 977(X) of December 15, 1955** as sovereign UN-administered territory.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: UNMCK is **the most globally significant Korean War heritage site in Busan** and a sober opener to the day's coastal/cultural circuit. The 09:30 small-group arrival catches it before the 11:00 tour-bus peak — important for a fixed-itinerary group, since cemetery atmosphere degrades rapidly with crowds. Wheelchair-accessibility makes it the lowest-mobility stop of the day; the Wall of Remembrance and 12-min documentary deliver depth without strenuous walking, which suits the small-group's mixed mobility levels. For cruise guests on tight reboarding windows the 60-min dwell is the day's most schedule-flexible stop — visitors can compress to ~30 min while still covering documentary + Symbolic Area + Wall, or extend to 75 min for the Sculpture Park.

convenience.restroom: At the Visitor Center and Memorial Service Hall
convenience.parking: Free on-site parking + tour-coach drop-off zone

smartNotes.photo: **Wall of Remembrance** (40,896 names on 140 marble panels), **Symbolic Area** with 22 sending-state flags, and the **Frank Gaylord US Korean War Monument (2013)** — same sculptor as the Washington DC National Mall memorial. Photography permitted; respectful silence requested.
smartNotes.tip: **Only UN cemetery in the world** under UN-administered protected territory (UN GA Resolution 977(X), 1955). The annual **'Turn Toward Busan' ceremony on November 11 at 11:00 AM** is the only time all 22 sending-state flags lower in unison.

visitBasics.closed: Open year-round (Memorial Service Hall closed during ceremonies)
visitBasics.admission: FREE admission
visitBasics.walking: 100% wheelchair-accessible — flat paved paths across the 14-hectare grounds

category: Alpine Wetland — Ramsar Site + within Hallasan UNESCO World Heritage

en_fields.description: **Hallasan 1100 Altitude Wetland (한라산 1100고지 습지)** is a high-elevation alpine wetland system on the Hallasan plateau, located **at exactly 1,100 m above sea level** along **Route 1100 — the highest national highway in Korea** linking Jeju City to Seogwipo Jungmun. The site is a **Ramsar Wetland of International Importance (Site No. 1893, designated 12 October 2009)**, falling within the **Hallasan UNESCO World Heritage Site (2007)**, **Jeju Island UNESCO Biosphere Reserve (2002)**, and **Hallasan National Park (1970)** — making this one of the few sites globally with **four overlapping international/national designations**. The wetland comprises **16+ discontinuous freshwater marshes and pools** spread across a low-gradient plateau section that captures and retains rainwater, forming **small permanent lakes and peat bogs** that support diverse floristic assemblages with significant Jeju endemism. Recorded biodiversity includes **3 threatened bird species and 1 threatened plant species within Korea**, plus mammal, reptile, and insect species endemic to Jeju Island. The site received **900,000 visitors in 2008** (Mt Halla Visitor Centre data). Visitor experience: a **1-km wooden boardwalk loop** with observation decks, interpretive signage, and a small natural-history exhibit at the trailhead. **Autumn foliage (October–November)** and **winter snow scenes (December–February)** are the most photographed seasons; **spring + summer** offer wetland flora and endemic species visibility. **FREE admission**; adjacent **1100 Rest Area (1100 휴게소)** provides indoor restrooms + cafe + small souvenir shop. **Cultural and religious importance** to local communities is documented in the Ramsar listing — historical shaman pilgrimages tied to mountain-water spirits.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: **Alpine UNESCO opening anchor of the west-south authentic route**. Where the south-UNESCO sister tour (Tour 1) opens at Hallasan's **lower-elevation Eoseungsaengak trail** (1,169 m), this authentic-emphasis route opens at the **higher 1,100 m wetland** for a **fundamentally different Hallasan experience**: not a cone-climb but an **alpine wetland boardwalk** showcasing Jeju endemism + Ramsar-grade biodiversity. Route 1100 itself is the experience — Korea's highest national highway, with a winding ascent through Hallasan's western flank delivering panoramic foliage + snow scenes depending on season. Compared with **Yeongsil Trail (popular but 4.5-hour round-trip summit route)** or **Eorimok Trail (longer mid-elevation)**, the 1100 Wetland is **45-minute viable** and delivers UNESCO + Ramsar + Biosphere depth in a single boardwalk loop — the highest yield-per-minute alpine stop in Hallasan National Park.

convenience.restroom: Available at visitor center

smartNotes.photo: Boardwalk leading into the wetland with Hallasan in the distance is the iconic shot; autumn (October) gives golden grass colors

category: Hallasan Foothill Trail (Eorimok) — UNESCO World Heritage Taste

en_fields.description: **Eoseungsaengak (어승생악, 1,169 m)** is a parasitic cone (오름 / *oreum*) on the western flank of **Hallasan (한라산, 1,950 m)** — Korea's highest peak and the geological heart of Jeju. Hallasan itself carries an **unparalleled designation stack**: **UNESCO World Natural Heritage (inscribed 2007-07-02)**, **UNESCO Biosphere Reserve (2002)**, **Korea National Park (designated 1970-03-24)**, and **Natural Monument No. 182 (1966)** — making it the only Korean site holding all three UNESCO natural designations simultaneously. The Eoseungsaengak trailhead at **Eorimok Visitor Center** climbs **only ~200 m over 1.3 km** to a wide summit deck — a 60-minute round trip that delivers the **'Hallasan UNESCO taste'** without the full 9-hour Baengnokdam crater ascent. From the deck on clear days you see the **Yellow Sea, Hallasan's Baengnokdam summit caldera (백록담), and the parasitic cone field** stretching south. Botanical highlight: this elevation band (1,000–1,300 m) is the **transition zone between deciduous broadleaf forest and the Korean fir (구상나무, *Abies koreana* — IUCN Near Threatened, endemic to South Korean high mountains) belt** above. Hallasan harbors **2,000+ plant species** total — roughly **half of all plants on the Korean Peninsula**. A historical footnote sits at the summit deck: a **1945 Imperial Japanese tochika (토치카, concrete pillbox/communication post)** built in the war's final months — one of the highest surviving WWII Japanese fortifications in Korea. Admission and parking are **free**; **no reservation required** for Eoseungsaengak (unlike Baengnokdam summit which caps daily climbers at 1,000).
en_fields.whyOnRoute: **Opening anchor of the southwest loop**. Starting at Hallasan establishes Jeju's geological foundation, but unlike the highlights-loop sister tour (which carries guests counter-clockwise to the east coast), this tour pivots **west and southwest** after the descent — heading directly to Jusangjeolli on the southern coast, then Cheonjeyeon (also south), inland to Osulloc tea fields, and finally **west to Aewol for the sunset finale**. The 09:00 earlier start (vs. 09:30 sister) recovers ~30 min for the leisurely afternoon Aewol coastal walk + cafe stop sequence. Eoseungsaengak is the **only Hallasan trail giving UNESCO World Heritage + Biosphere Reserve + Geopark in 60 minutes** without reservation hassle — Yeongsil and Seongpanak both require 5–9 hours, and Baengnokdam summit caps daily entry at 1,000.

convenience.restroom: At the Eorimok entrance area

en_fields.description: Lunch is at a coach-friendly local restaurant en route from Haeundae back across central Busan toward UN Memorial Cemetery in Nam-gu. Two typical options depending on coach routing and group preference: (1) **Dwaeji Gukbap (돼지국밥)** — Busan's signature pork-bone broth rice soup, the city's working-class comfort food since the Korean War refugee era, served with kkakdugi radish kimchi, saeujeot shrimp paste, buchu chives, and rice (~9,000-12,000 KRW per bowl); recommended in the Seomyeon or Daeyeon-dong neighborhoods, or (2) **Milmyeon (밀면)** — Busan's wheat-flour cold noodles, an adaptation of North Korean Pyongyang naengmyeon by refugees who couldn't find buckwheat in wartime Busan, served in chilled beef-broth or in spicy bibim style (~8,000-11,000 KRW). Both are **regional specialties unique to Busan and not commonly found in Seoul**. Lunch is at the guest's own expense; vegetarian and halal-friendly options are limited at most dwaeji-gukbap restaurants — please notify the guide at booking if dietary accommodations are needed and a separate restaurant (typically a bibimbap or hansik buffet) will be substituted. Total time ~60 min including parking + ordering + eating + restroom + boarding the coach.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: Lunch is timed at 13:00 to bridge the morning's east-coast cluster (Haedong Yonggungsa + Cheongsapo) and the afternoon's central Busan cluster (UN Memorial Cemetery + Gamcheon + Jagalchi). The choice of dwaeji-gukbap or milmyeon over generic Korean BBQ reflects the route's local-culture priority — both dishes are Busan-specific Korean War heritage cuisine that guests are unlikely to encounter on a Seoul-only Korea trip. Restaurant choice on the day depends on coach routing and current restaurant availability; tour guide will confirm at the morning briefing.

Both dishes have Korean War refugee origins unique to Busan

en_fields.description: Lunch is at a coach-friendly Gyeongju restaurant en route between Daereungwon and Bulguksa. The most distinctive local options: (1) **Gyeongju Ssambap (쌈밥)** — a Gyeongju-signature 'wrap-and-eat' set meal where a charcoal-grilled pork or beef main is wrapped in 10+ varieties of leafy greens (perilla, lettuce, cabbage, kkaennip, sesame leaves) with assorted ban-chan side dishes and dipping pastes (~₩13,000-18,000 per set), (2) **Hwangnam-bbang (황남빵)** — Gyeongju's most famous traditional pastry, a small disc of sweet red-bean paste in a thin wheat shell, originally made by Choi Yeong-hwa in the Hwangnam neighborhood since 1939, perfect as a quick lunch supplement (~₩1,500-2,000 each), or (3) **Gyodong Beopju (교동법주)** rice wine + traditional set menu at Gyochon Hanok Village's Yosukgung 1779 — the heritage Choi-clan recipe rice wine is **Korea's Important Intangible Cultural Asset No. 86-3** (~₩30,000-50,000 set, premium option). Lunch is at the guest's own expense; vegetarian + halal-friendly options are limited at ssambap restaurants — please notify the guide at booking if dietary accommodations are needed and a substitute (typically a bibimbap or hansik buffet) will be arranged. Total time ~60 min including parking + meal + restroom + boarding.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: Lunch is timed at 13:00 to bridge the morning's downtown-Gyeongju cluster (Bomun Lake + Cheomseongdae/Daereungwon) and the afternoon's Bulguksa visit (~30 min drive east to Tohamsan). Choosing Gyeongju ssambap or hwangnam-bbang over generic Korean BBQ reflects the route's local-heritage priority — both are Gyeongju-specific dishes that guests are unlikely to encounter elsewhere in Korea.

en_fields.description: **Pickup is consolidated at four central Jeju City points** to minimize morning crisscrossing: **Jeju International Airport (제주공항)** for guests with same-day arrivals; **Jeju City Hall (제주시청)** for the Yeon-dong / Nohyeong / Old Town hotel cluster; **Hagwi-dong (하귀동)** as the westernmost pickup before the southbound stretch; and **Dongmun Traditional Market (동문시장)** as the optional eastern Jeju City pickup. Sequence is Hagwi → Airport → City Hall → Dongmun → southbound Route 1100 toward the Hallasan 1100 Altitude Wetland trailhead. The minicoach is **small-group (max ~14 pax)** with English-speaking guide. **Bilingual itinerary card** distributed at pickup, weather + Route 1100 closure brief at first stop. **Route 1100 closure check** is performed before departure — if closed for snow/ice, the 1100 Wetland stop substitutes to a lower-elevation alternate (typically Mysterious Road or Andeok Valley).
en_fields.whyOnRoute: Four pickup points cover Jeju City's hotel zones (airport, mid-city, old town, market district) without forcing guests across the city. Sequencing west→east lands the coach at 1100 Wetland ~10:20 — early enough to catch the morning alpine clarity before midday haze. **Route 1100 closure protocol** ensures guests are informed about winter contingency before departure — Jeju's only highest-altitude national highway is closed ~5–10 days/year for ice.

en_fields.description: Pickup from one of two central Seoul meeting points: **Hongik University Station Exit 8** (Hongdae area, Seoul Subway Line 2/Airport Express) or **Myeongdong Station Exit 2 or 4** (Seoul Subway Line 4). Confirm exact pickup time and location at booking — the schedule is built around an early start to reach Hwaseong Fortress before the late-morning Korean tour-group rush. About **1 hour by coach south to Suwon** via the Gyeongbu Expressway — the historical fortress city of Joseon's King Jeongjo, who built Hwaseong (1794-1796) to honor and protect the tomb of his executed father Crown Prince Sado. Light traffic Mon-Fri morning; weekend traffic adds 15-20 min cushion. Coach is air-conditioned and equipped with USB charging at most seats.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: An 09:00 Seoul departure puts the group at Hwaseong Fortress before midday crowds and aligns with Korean Folk Village's afternoon performance schedule (Hanbok wedding ceremony reenactment, equestrian show, Nong-ak farmers' percussion).

smartNotes.tip: Confirm exact pickup time and meeting point at booking — Seoul morning rush hour (07:00-09:00) makes punctuality critical. Bring water, sunscreen, and comfortable shoes for the fortress wall walking.

1-hour coach south to Suwon via Gyeongbu Expressway

en_fields.description: **Hwaseong Haenggung (화성행궁) — Korea's largest existing haenggung (detached palace)** — was built 1789-1796 under King Jeongjo as part of the unified Hwaseong fortress city plan. A **haenggung** is a temporary/detached royal residence used by Joseon kings during travel, war refuge, or special ceremonies; Hwaseong Haenggung surpasses Onyang, Bukhansan, and Namhansan haenggung in scale. King Jeongjo built it primarily to stay overnight when visiting his executed father Crown Prince Sado's tomb (Hyeollyungwon, now called Yungneung Royal Tomb, 4 km south) — a pilgrimage he made **13 times during his reign**. The most famous of these journeys was the **1795 royal procession** with 6,000 attendants and 1,400 horses, celebrating the 60th birthday of Lady Hyegyeong (the king's mother and Sado's widow). The 1795 procession is documented in the **Wonhaeng Eulmyo Jeongni Uigwe — UNESCO Memory of the World register since 2007**. The palace originally comprised **576 kan (rooms/structural bays)** functioning as a complete miniature royal residence with throne hall (Bongsudang), secondary hall (Naknamheon), residence quarters (Janganheon), kitchens, military offices, and guards' quarters. **Largely destroyed during Japanese colonial period** (1910-1945) when the Japanese converted the palace site into Suwon Provincial Hospital and Police Station. **Restoration began 1996** following the original Hwaseong Seongyeok Uigwe construction record; first phase complete 2003 (Bongsudang, Naknamheon, Janganheon, Yuyeotaek), second phase complete 2009 (Wuhwagwan royal kitchen, Sinpung-nu entrance gate). Restoration continues today. **Critical historical note: Naknamheon (낙남헌) is the ONLY building that survived from the original 1796 construction** — Japanese converted it into Police Station offices but did not demolish it. It is therefore the only AUTHENTIC original wood-and-tile structure on the site; all other buildings are reconstructions. **Daily Royal Guard Changing Ceremony** at Sinpungnu Gate — **Tuesday-Sunday 11:00 AM** (weather permitting), ~15 min, FREE. Recreates the Joseon-era palace gate ceremony with guards in red, blue, and yellow uniforms with traditional drums and horns. The tour times arrival for the 11:00 ceremony when day-of-week permits. Filmed for K-drama **'Jewel in the Palace' (Daejanggeum, 2003)** — the iconic Joseon-era hit with 50 million domestic viewers — making this one of Korea's most-filmed sageuk locations.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: Pairs naturally with the fortress wall — the temporary royal residence is INSIDE the same UNESCO site, 5 minutes apart. Korea's largest haenggung (576 rooms originally), with **Naknamheon the only authentic original structure** (the others are post-1996 reconstructions following the Hwaseong Uigwe). Time arrival for the Royal Guard Changing Ceremony (Tue-Sun 11:00 AM) which is FREE and ~15 min — one of the catalog's strongest free cultural-experience hooks. Visiting Haenggung immediately after the fortress walls converts an abstract military-architecture lesson into lived royal-Joseon spatial experience — the same Bongsudang throne hall where King Jeongjo received officials in 1795 also hosted the lavish 60th-birthday banquet for his mother Lady Hyegyeong (depicted in the Banchado court-painting scrolls preserved in the Uigwe). A **Combined Fortress + Haenggung ticket** is sold at 3,500 KRW (vs 2,500 KRW separately) — a tangible value-added anchor for the Suwon stop. The Royal Guard Changing Ceremony at Sinpungnu Gate (Tue-Sun 11:00 AM, ~15 min, FREE) is timed to align with most Suwon-arrival schedules from a Seoul-departing day-trip.

Skip Mondays — Royal Guard Changing Ceremony performed Tue-Sun only.

en_fields.description: Bukchon literally means 'Northern Village' — a 600-year-old hanok (traditional Korean tile-roofed house) residential district nestled between Gyeongbokgung Palace and Changdeokgung Palace, originally home to high-ranking Joseon Dynasty government officials and aristocracy. Today Bukchon is Seoul's largest concentration of preserved hanok homes — approximately 900 traditional houses arrayed along eight famous viewpoints (Bukchon 8 Gyeong / Bukchon 8 Views) — with sloping alleys giving views of N Seoul Tower in the distance and the curved tile-roof rooflines layering down toward the city below. The signature view is Gye-dong-gil (Hanok Roofline Alley near Bukchon No. 6 viewpoint) where photographers gather for the iconic shot of cascading hanok rooftops with N Seoul Tower in the distance. Many hanok along the route are private residences (residents have asked tourists to stay quiet — signs request silence after 22:00 and limited photography of doorways). Cultural workshops scattered through the village offer hands-on experiences in Korean knot-tying, hanji (mulberry paper) crafts, traditional tea ceremonies, embroidery, and Korean cooking. Hanbok rental shops cluster at the village edge (~15,000-20,000 KRW for 4 hours) — the standard photo-trip pairing with Gyeongbokgung. Insadong, the cultural and crafts shopping street, sits a 5-10 minute walk south. The village's preservation is overseen by **Jongno-gu District Office's Bukchon Hanok Village preservation program** — strict zoning rules limit commercial signage, renovation must use traditional materials (handmade hanji paper screens, wooden frames, tile roofs), and resident-quiet hours 22:00–10:00 are enforced by signage and community wardens during peak season.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: Bukchon is the natural pivot from Gyeongbokgung's grand-scale royal architecture to a domestic-scale Joseon-era streetscape — completing the 'how kings AND aristocrats lived 500 years ago' narrative within walking distance. Picked over Namsangol Hanok Village (a curated open-air museum) because Bukchon is a LIVING village with residents — far more authentic for the story-driven cruise passenger window. For cruise guests on a one-day-in-Seoul window, Bukchon delivers the day's strongest 'period-set' photo opportunity — hanok rooflines + Insadong-bound alley vistas + the Bukchon 8 Gyeong viewpoints make this a 75-min Instagram-grade stop within walking distance of both Gyeongbokgung and Insadong.

convenience.restroom: Tourist Information Center at Bukchon entrance; cafes scattered through the village

smartNotes.photo: **Bukchon 8 Gyeong (8 Famous Views)** — the #6 viewpoint at Gye-dong-gil for the iconic hanok-roofline-and-N-Seoul-Tower shot. Hanbok-wearers framed against tile-roof gables. Ssamzigil cultural alley (covered alley with shops at the south edge near Insadong).
smartNotes.facilities: Tourist Information Center near Anguk Station Exit 2 with multilingual maps. Coffee shops, traditional teahouses, hanok-converted cafes scattered through the village. Photo zones marked.
smartNotes.tip: **Visit before noon or after 16:00** — residents have publicly requested quieter visits; some lanes have signs prohibiting tour groups during early-morning resident hours. Stay quiet and on the marked photo paths. Hanbok rental shops at the village edge — Hanboknam, Oneday Hanbok, etc. Combine with Insadong (5 min walk) and Gyeongbokgung (10 min walk) for a Seoul-northside half-day.
