name_en: Tangerine (Gyul) Picking Experience — Winter Hallabong Peak

en_fields.description: **Jeju tangerine (gyul / 감귤) picking experience** at a guide-vetted farm in the **Seogwipo / Namwon-eup / Jungmun area**, selected for the day's specific date based on **variety calendar** — December–January is **Hallabong (한라봉) peak** (mid-Dec to mid-Jan window), Jeju's most famous easy-peel hybrid. **Jeju citrus has been gifted seasonally to Korean royalty since the Baekje period (4th–7th century) through Silla and Goryeo dynasties** — **a cultivation tradition over 1,500 years old** that is still anchored to Jeju's volcanic soil + warm-temperate climate. **Standard format**: the farm provides a basket and scissors (some include gloves); **eat-as-you-pick is unlimited on-farm** (tasting all you want), and guests purchase take-home weight by the kg or as part of the entry fee. **Typical pricing**: **₩5,000–15,000 per person entry** (often includes 1 kg take-home), **+₩2,500–₩15,000 per additional kg** depending on variety. **Citrus calendar by season**: Spring (Mar–May) Hallabong / Jinjihyang / Seminole; Summer (Jun–Aug) greenhouse tangerines + blueberries; Fall (Sep–Nov) early-harvest Noji; **Winter Peak (Dec–Jan) Noji + Hallabong**; Late Winter (Feb) Hallabong + Cheonhyehyang. **Featured varieties**: Hallabong (most famous, juicy/sweet/easy-peel; Dec–Jan peak), Cheonhyehyang (large hybrid tangerine + orange), Hwanggeumhyang (Hallabong + Cheonhyehyang cross), Red Hyang (red-orange flesh, Hallabong + Noji blend), Hagyul (sour, citrus-ade base), Dangyuja (tangy heritage cultivar). **Vetted farms in the rotation**: **Gyulhyangi Experience Farm** (₩7,000 entry incl 1 kg, English-friendly staff, complimentary tangerine tea + biscuit + jam tasting); **Bong Bong Tangerine Farm** (Namwon-eup, ₩5,000 entry + ₩2,500/kg, on-site cafe, named after owner's golden retriever); **Choenamdan Experience Farm** (monorail ride to orchards, Hallabong + Cheonhyehyang); **Jeju Southernmost Theme Park** (GAP-certified, year-round greenhouse picking, monorail, petting zoo with rabbits/deer/ducks/black pigs); **Jejue In Farm** (cafe + orchard combo, Hallabong-ade signature drink). **Rain backup**: most farms have greenhouse sections — winter rain or unexpected weather rarely cancels the stop. **Family-friendly**: easy flat orchard paths, suitable for all ages and stroller-accessible at most farms; children under 3 cannot ride monorail at Choenamdan / Southernmost.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: **Tangerine picking is the day's signature winter agritourism anchor** — and the schedule is built around the **Hallabong peak window (mid-Dec to mid-Jan)** that gives this tour its name. Positioning it **third in the day** (12:00 after Hallasan, before lunch) takes advantage of guests' post-hike appetite — eat-as-you-pick is generous, and many guests take 5–10 tangerines on-farm before lunch arrives. The Seogwipo / Namwon-eup farm cluster is also **15–25 min from Hallasan trailhead and 10 min from Camellia Hill** (the next stop), keeping the day's geographic flow tight. **Variety choice is date-driven**: the operator selects Hallabong-peaking farms in late Dec–early Jan, Hallabong-fading + Cheonhyehyang-rising farms in late Jan–Feb, and year-round greenhouse-equipped farms (Jeju Southernmost) for off-window dates — **giving the tour a stable picking experience across the entire Dec–Feb winter window**, not just peak weeks. Take-home tangerines also serve as a tangible, edible souvenir — competitively priced vs city-market or Olle Maeil Market boxes.
highlights: **Jeju citrus tradition over 1,500 years old** — royal gift since Baekje period (4th–7th c)
**Hallabong peak mid-Dec to mid-Jan** — most famous variety, easy-peel + juicy + sweet
**Eat-as-you-pick unlimited on-farm**; take-home weighed at ₩2,500–15,000/kg
**Entry ₩5,000–15,000/pax** (often includes 1 kg take-home)
**7 featured varieties**: Hallabong / Cheonhyehyang / Hwanggeumhyang / Red Hyang / Gamgyul / Hagyul / Dangyuja
**Gyulhyangi Farm** — English-friendly staff + complimentary tangerine tea/jam tasting
**Jeju Southernmost Theme Park** — GAP-certified, year-round greenhouse, monorail + petting zoo
**Family-friendly** — flat orchard paths, stroller-accessible, kid-favorite

category: King Cherry Blossom corridor — Jeju City

en_fields.description: **Jeonnong-ro (전농로)** is **the oldest tree-lined cherry blossom street in Jeju** — a **1.2 km festival corridor** in central Jeju City lined with **hundreds of King Cherry trees (왕벚나무, *wangbeotnamu*), some over 100 years old**. The King Cherry is **native to Jeju Island** and **was first documented on Hallasan Mountain in 1908** by French missionary Émile Taquet — the variety is genetically distinct from Japan's Yoshino cherries (a topic of ongoing scholarly debate between Korean and Japanese botanists). King Cherry petals are **noticeably larger and fluffier than Yoshino petals**, with a richer pink tone. Peak bloom lasts only **3–5 days, typically March 25–30** (in 2026, the Korea Tourism Organization confirmed bloom **2–7 days earlier than historical average** due to a warmer-than-usual winter — first blooms expected around March 25). The **annual Jeju King Cherry Blossom Festival** (제주 왕벚꽃축제) — **19th edition in 2026** — closes the 1.2 km corridor to traffic, hangs **red and blue lanterns** along the trees, and runs nighttime illumination. **Address: 40 Jeonnong-ro, Jeju-si**; FREE admission (public street); festival hours typically 10:00–22:00 with stage events. Easy 30–60 min stroll suitable for strollers and seniors. The **Samseonghyeol founding-myth shrine** (legendary cave of Jeju's three founding gods) is within walking distance — its cherry trees lean toward the cave as if paying homage. The festival's car-free corridor runs **~2 weeks during peak bloom**, with red Korean traditional lanterns (cheongsachorong, 청사초롱) hung along the entire 1.2 km stretch and **night illumination from 18:00–22:00** lighting the cherry tunnels for evening photography — a feature **unavailable at any other Korean cherry blossom site**. Vendor stalls (jeon, tteokbokki, hotteok, makgeolli) and traditional drum performances on weekends throughout the festival window create a **two-tier daytime + evening** photo experience.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: Jeonnong-ro is **the catalog-leading anchor for any Jeju cherry blossom tour** — the densest concentration of native King Cherry trees in Korea, in a flat 1.2 km festival corridor. Starting the day here at 09:00 catches the **morning soft light + the lowest crowd density** before tour buses arrive en masse around 11:00. Selected over **Jeju University Cherry Blossom Avenue** (also famous, but campus access restricted on weekdays) and **Halla Arboretum** (botanical mix, smaller cherry concentration). The morning slot (09:00 arrival before tour-bus surge at 11:00) is **catalog-best timing** for low-density photography — and pairs perfectly with the rural Noksan-ro afternoon, providing a **two-temperament cherry experience (urban festival density + rural pastoral solitude)** unavailable on any other Jeju route. The 70-min dwell allows full corridor walk-through plus festival vendor sampling without rushing. Tour 1 of 1 in this single-tour batch — the catalog's spring-only east-Jeju anchor.

convenience.restroom: Nearby cafes

smartNotes.photo: Morning low light gives the softest blossom glow.
smartNotes.facilities: Use restroom at pickup before leaving city.
smartNotes.tip: Cherry timing window is 7-10 days only — go on day 1 of your trip if possible.

en_fields.description: **Haeundae Blue Line Park (해운대 블루라인파크)** is an eco-friendly redevelopment of the **abandoned Donghae Nambu coastal railway**, opened to the public in **2020**. The complex spans 4.8 km of Busan's most photogenic coastline between **Mipo Station** (near Haeundae Beach) and **Songjeong Station** (near Songjeong Beach), with three ways to experience the same scenery: (1) the **Sky Capsule** — pastel-colored 4-person cabins on an elevated rail 7-10 meters above the coast, running 2.3 km between Mipo and Cheongsapo, (2) the **Beach Train** — a battery-powered eco-train with full-glass ocean-side benches running the entire 4.8 km on 6 stops (Mipo → Dalmaji Tunnel → Cheongsapo → Daritdol Skywalk → Gudeokpo → Songjeong), and (3) the **Busan Green Railway** — a 4.8 km wooden coastal boardwalk that won the 2021 Ministry of Land 'Korea Landscaping Award'. Today's stop centers on **Cheongsapo (청사포)** — a small fishing-village station that's one of Busan's eight scenic views — with two free highlight attractions: (1) the **Cheongsapo Daritdol Skywalk**, opened **August 2017**, a 72.5-m-long, 20-m-high half-moon-shaped glass-bottom observatory in the shape of a blue dragon, FREE admission with slipper covers provided to protect the glass, and (2) the **Cheongsapo Twin Lighthouses** (red + white) and the famous **station crosswalk** that's gone viral for its Slam Dunk anime resemblance. Guests have free time to (a) ride the **Sky Capsule one-way Mipo→Cheongsapo (₩30,000 for 2 ppl, ₩39,000 for 3, ₩44,000 for 4)** if booked in advance, or (b) walk the wooden Green Railway ~30 min from Mipo to Cheongsapo. **Sky Capsule reservations should be made through the official bluelinepark.com or Klook 1-2 weeks in advance** for sunset slots; tour staff can assist with on-site automated kiosks if available.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: The route's **most-Instagrammed** segment — the pastel Sky Capsule has become a viral Korea-travel symbol since 2020. Cheongsapo is chosen over Mipo or Songjeong because the Daritdol Skywalk + Twin Lighthouses + village atmosphere all sit within a 600-m walking radius of the station, fitting cleanly inside a 75-min stop. Sky Capsule is OPTIONAL because the on-the-day boarding queue can run 30+ min and would otherwise jeopardize the lunch + UN Cemetery + Gamcheon timing later in the day; guests who must ride should book the sunset Mipo→Cheongsapo slot independently.

en_fields.description: **Haedong Yonggungsa (해동 용궁사) — Korea's most famous seaside Buddhist temple** — perches on the rocky cliffs of Gijang-gun on Busan's northeastern coast, one of the very few major Korean temples NOT set in the mountains. Originally founded in **1376 during the Goryeo Dynasty by the great Buddhist teacher Naong Hyegeun (나옹 혜근, 1320-1376)** — a royal consultant and master who would help shape Korean Buddhism into the early Joseon era. Per legend, Naong was practicing asceticism at Bunhwangsa Temple in Gyeongju during a national drought; the East Sea god appeared to him in a dream and told him that building a temple on Bongrae Mountain (the original name of this seaside cliff) would restore the kingdom's prosperity. Naong founded the temple and named it **Bomunsa**, dedicated to Gwanseum-bosal (관세음보살, the Bodhisattva of Compassion). The temple was destroyed in the **Japanese Imjin Invasion of 1592** and lay in ruins for over 300 years until **Monk Ungang of Tongdosa Temple began reconstruction in the early 1930s**. The current name, **Haedong Yonggungsa (Korean Dragon Palace Temple)**, dates only to **1974** — when Head Monk Jeong-am, during a 100-day prayer (jeong-geon kido), had a vision of the Goddess of Mercy descending on the back of a dragon, emanating multicolor light. The temple was renamed to honor this vision. The visit follows the temple's signature processional path: through the row of **12 Sibiji-shin (Twelve Zodiac Generals)** at the entry plaza, past the **7-story 'No-Accident Pagoda'** (where drivers leave coin offerings under a stone wheel-shrine), down the famous **108 stone stairs** (representing the 108 delusions of the Buddhist mind) through a small bamboo grove, and out onto the seaside terraces and Daeungjeon main hall. The **Haesu Gwaneum Daebul** (Sea-Goddess of Mercy statue) on the upper cliff is the most photographed view, framing both the temple rooftops and the East Sea. Hours **04:30-19:20 daily, last entry 18:50, FREE admission**. **Buddha's Birthday (May)** brings Yeondunhoe lantern festival displays.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: The route's iconic anchor — the most-photographed temple in Busan, with a 650-year history and a rare Korean seaside Buddhist setting. Picking the 10:00 arrival window lands before the major tour-bus surge (typically 11:00 onward) so the 108-stair descent and the Haesu Gwaneum viewpoint are still walkable without queuing. The northeast-coast location also pairs naturally with Cheongsapo Blue Line Park immediately south — together forming the day's east-coast cluster before the coach swings back across the city for UN Memorial Cemetery, Gamcheon, and Jagalchi.

en_fields.description: **Eorimok Trail** (어리목 탐방로) is one of **two easiest of Hallasan National Park's 5 trails** (the others requiring advance reservation include Seongpanak and Gwaneumsa for summit access — Eorimok and Yeongsil are the **non-summit, no-reservation routes** that are always available). The trail leads **4.7 km one-way to Witseoreum Shelter (1,700 m elevation)**, the official turnaround at the Witseoreum plateau — **NOT the summit** (1,950 m Baengnokdam crater is reservation-only via Seongpanak/Gwaneumsa). For a **9-hour day-tour**, the standard winter-season practice is to hike **1.5–2 km in** (~45 min one-way) to reach the **forest-to-plateau transition** where dwarf bamboo fields open up to dramatic Hallasan-summit-wall views, then return to the trailhead — covering ~3–4 km total in ~90 min hiking time + ~30 min trail buffer. **Winter conditions** (November–March): the trail's most dramatic season — **snow-encrusted dwarf trees create a 'frozen guardian' landscape** widely photographed by Korean hiking clubs who plan last-minute trips after fresh snowfalls; **hoarfrost** + occasional **sea-of-clouds below the ridge** + mountain-side snow drifts make this Jeju's signature winter photo. **Crampons recommended November–March** (basic strap-on sets sold at the trailhead ~₩8,000 / ~$8 USD; the operator also carries 2–3 spare sets in-vehicle). **FREE admission** (Hallasan National Park has no entry fee); **opens 05:00, last entry ~11:30 in winter** (vs ~12:30 other seasons — the early winter cutoff is the day's hard schedule constraint). **25 min drive from Jeju City** via Route 1100; free trailhead car park (fills on weekends — the operator schedules early arrival in peak season). Bus access also available via **Bus 240** from Jeju Bus Terminal.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: **Hallasan Eorimok is the day's altitude + winter-snow anchor** — Jeju's defining winter landscape happens above 800 m, and Eorimok delivers it at moderate hiking effort (1.5–2 km tour-group walk) without the summit-route reservation constraint that excludes most international cruise/day-trip itineraries. The trail's **forest-to-plateau transition** at ~1.5 km in is precisely where Jeju's most-photographed winter scene unfolds — **snow-encrusted dwarf bamboo + 'frozen guardian' trees + Hallasan summit wall up-close** — and it's the only stop in the catalog that captures this. Pacing the Hallasan stop **first in the day** also serves the operational schedule: the **~11:30 winter last-entry cutoff** at Eorimok is non-negotiable, so any other stop sequence would force a sub-90-min hike window or risk missing the cutoff entirely on a delayed pickup day. FREE admission also keeps the day's per-pax cost basis competitive against other winter Jeju tours.

category: Cherry + canola country drive — Joseon horse pastures

en_fields.description: **Noksan-ro (녹산로)** in **Gasi-ri Village (가시리, Pyoseon-myeon, Seogwipo-si)** is **'Jeju's Most Beautiful Spring Road'** and one of **Korea's '100 Most Beautiful Roads'** — a **10 km rural drive where King Cherry blossoms (pink) and canola flowers (yellow) bloom simultaneously**, a color combination found **nowhere else in Korea**. The road passes through the historic **Noksanjang and Gammajang horse pastures** — **the best royal horse-breeding farms of the Joseon Dynasty**, with **600 years of nomadic culture** rooted in this volcanic upland. Today **Jeju Horse Park (Jorangmal Experience Center)** at the road's center preserves this heritage with the **first community-run national museum in Korea (Jeju Horse Museum)**, **Ttarabi horseback riding track**, **Maeum Cafe**, **Mongol Yurt Guesthouse**, and a **camping site**. The road also runs past **Gasiri Wind Power Plant** (modern wind turbines), **Ttarabi Oreum** + **Keunsasumi Oreum** (parasitic volcanic cones), and **'Yujae-kkot Plaza' (canola flower square)**. Canola peak overlaps cherry blossom peak for ~5–7 days, typically **March 29–April 1**. **Jeju filming heritage**: scenes from **'When Life Gives You Tangerines' (2024 Netflix), 'Warm and Cozy' (2015 KBS), 'Welcome to Samdal-ri' (2023 JTBC)** were shot in the surrounding canola fields. **No public bus access** — the road is **charter-only or rental-car territory**, which keeps crowd density notably lower than Jeonnong-ro. The 600-year horse-pasture heritage adds a **deeper cultural dimension** missing from any pure-flower cherry blossom road in Korea — guests photograph cherry-canola tunnels with **Joseon-era stone walls (batdam, 밭담)** and **grazing horses** as background elements unique to Jeju's pastoral east. **Sunset alignment** (16:00–17:30 westward views toward Hallasan) is photogenic during peak bloom days.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: Noksan-ro is **Jeju's signature cherry-blossom drive experience** — fundamentally different from the urban Jeonnong-ro festival (which is a static walk corridor). Pairing the two on the same morning lets guests experience **both the urban festival density AND the rural pastoral solitude** of Jeju cherry season — a contrast unavailable on any other east-Jeju route. The 90-min dwell allows multiple photo stops along the 10 km drive. Noksan-ro's **drive-only access** (no public bus, not on KTX or any rail line) is a **deliberate strength** of this tour, not a limitation — it filters out non-driver mass tourism and preserves the low-density experience that makes the 10 km corridor photographable without crowd-management compromises. The 90-min dwell window is also calibrated to the **Korean cherry blossom bloom forecast** so guests booking 2-7 days early-season (per 2026 KMA forecast) still hit 80%+ peak overlap.

convenience.restroom: Limited rural facilities

smartNotes.photo: Wide compositions catch the layered pink/yellow effect.
smartNotes.facilities: Use restroom before this stop — rural section.
smartNotes.tip: Best window is late March to early April; canola can outlast cherry by 1-2 weeks.

visitBasics.hours: Open access (daylight)
visitBasics.admission: Free
visitBasics.walking: Easy to moderate

en_fields.description: The most landmark-dense route option, anchored on Jeju's strongest UNESCO and K-drama coast. Seongsan Ilchulbong is the flagship — a 5,000-year-old hydrovolcanic tuff cone rising 182 m straight from the sea, inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage list in 2007 as part of 'Jeju Volcanic Island and Lava Tubes' and the single most-photographed landmark on the island. Optional add-ons include Udo Island, Jeju's largest offshore island, reached via a 15-minute ferry from Seongsan Port (round-trip ~8,900 KRW); Manjanggul Lava Tube — a 7.4-km UNESCO-listed lava cave with the world's tallest 7.6 m lava column at the end of its 1 km tourist section (IMPORTANT: Manjanggul has been closed for major maintenance since December 2023; reopening was scheduled by March 31, 2026 followed by inspections — verify status before scheduling); Seopjikoji, the coastal cape made famous by the 2003 K-drama 'All In' and with spring rapeseed flower fields; Seongeup Folk Village (a Joseon-era thatched-roof settlement); and Hamdeok Beach (turquoise water + black volcanic rocks). The driver-guide adapts the order based on weather, ferry schedules, and your group's pace, and handles all parking and admission queueing.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: The strongest route for first-time Jeju visitors who want maximum landmark recognition. Seongsan Ilchulbong delivers the island's iconic UNESCO sunrise-peak photograph, Manjanggul (when open) gives the route a distinct underground UNESCO experience that no other Jeju route can match, and Seopjikoji + Seongeup provide the K-drama and folk-culture layers. Heaviest UNESCO density of all four routes.

convenience.restroom: Available at all major stops
convenience.parking: Driver-guide handles all parking — rental-car restrictions on Udo are not a concern for charter passengers (vehicle stays at Seongsan Port)

smartNotes.photo: Seongsan summit at clear-weather morning is the strongest single photograph of the day; Seopjikoji's coastal cape works best in afternoon side-light. For Manjanggul (when open), the world's-tallest lava column at the end of the tourist section is the iconic shot.
smartNotes.facilities: Seongsan harbor and Hamdeok Beach are the strongest meal hubs; the East route has materially stronger food density than the West route. Haenyeo-prepared raw fish is the local seafood signature.
smartNotes.tip: The Seongsan summit climb is the route's bottleneck for senior or low-mobility travelers — base-area viewing is also valid. If Manjanggul is still closed at booking time, the driver-guide will substitute Sangumburi Crater or Hamdeok Beach. Time the Udo ferry at least 4 hours before sunset to be safe.

visitBasics.hours: Most stops 09:00-18:00. Manjanggul (when open) closes the first Wednesday of each month and 17:10 is last entry. Seongsan closes the first Monday of each month for protection.
visitBasics.closed: Seongsan: first Monday monthly. Manjanggul: first Wednesday monthly + currently closed for maintenance (re-confirm post-March 2026).
visitBasics.admission: Pay directly at each stop — Seongsan 5,000 KRW, Manjanggul 4,000 KRW, Seopjikoji 4,000 KRW, Udo round-trip ferry from ~8,900 KRW. Driver-guide assists with ticket purchase.
visitBasics.walking: Moderate to high — Seongsan summit climb is the most demanding section (~30-40 min round trip on stairs). Base-area viewing without the climb is also valid for low-mobility travelers.

highlights: Seongsan Ilchulbong: triple-UNESCO site (World Natural Heritage 2007 + Biosphere Reserve + Global Geopark) — 30-40 min summit climb to the 99-rock crater rim, summit closes the first Monday of each month; admission 5,000 KRW
Udo Island: 15-min ferry from Seongsan, ~370 residents, 132 m Udobong peak, signature peanut ice cream and white-coral-sand Hagosudong Beach; rental cars restricted unless overnight stay

Manjanggul Lava Tube: world's largest 7.6 m lava column, 1 km accessible passage of a 7.4 km UNESCO tube, year-round 11-13°C interior; admission 4,000 KRW; closed first Wednesday — verify post-March 2026 reopening status
Seopjikoji and Seongeup: K-drama 'All In' (2003) coast + Joseon-era thatched-roof folk village; Seongeup designated National Folklore Cultural Heritage

poi_meta.note: Route option for private charter — represents a multi-POI bundle (Seongsan, Udo, Manjanggul, Seopjikoji, Seongeup, Hamdeok); not a single matchable POI. All constituent POIs are individually verified in KB v1.10.

en_fields.description: The most family-oriented route, pairing South Korea's largest theme park with Korea's first open-air folk-culture museum — both in Yongin, ~36 km south of Seoul. Everland, opened in 1976 and operated by Samsung C&T, draws 5.85-6.6 million visitors annually (ranked 19th globally for amusement-park attendance in 2018) across five themed zones (Global Fair, American Adventure, Magic Land, Zootopia, European Adventure). The signature ride is T Express — Korea's first wooden roller coaster, opened March 2008 — with 1.64 km of track, a 46 m drop at 77° (one of the steepest wooden coasters in the world), and a 104 km/h top speed. Zootopia hosts ~2,000 animals across 200 species including the Aibao + Lebao panda family (Fubao, born 2020, was the first giant panda born in South Korea; her twin sisters Ruibao and Huibao followed in 2023). Korean Folk Village, just 20 minutes away on the same Yongin road, is a 991,735 m² (245-acre) site with 260+ traditional Joseon-era houses representing different regions and social classes. Daily performances include UNESCO-listed Jultagi tightrope walking, Nong-ak farmers' percussion music, mounted-acrobatic horseback martial arts, and reenacted Joseon weddings. Many K-dramas (Jewel in the Palace, Sungkyunkwan Scandal, etc.) have filmed sageuk scenes here. Because Everland alone fills a full day, the typical 10-hour charter does ONE of the two thoroughly rather than both — the driver-guide flags this at booking.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: The strongest route for families with kids 6-15, theme-park enthusiasts, and travelers wanting a single-location-deep day rather than multi-stop sightseeing. Everland alone is normally an 8-10 hour day — the private charter advantage is hotel-to-gate transit (~50-70 min each way) avoiding the bus-transfer chain. Folk Village alone fits comfortably in a half-day with strong cultural depth; combining both is realistic only for groups happy to sample rather than fully explore.

convenience.restroom: Multiple at both — well signposted
convenience.parking: Driver-guide handles all parking; both have dedicated paid lots (Folk Village 2,000 KRW car / 3,000 SUV)

smartNotes.photo: Everland — T Express drop and the seasonal Tulip Festival (Mar-Apr) Romantic Tower Tree (26 m flower sculpture); Folk Village — yangban two-story house with autumn ginkgo (last two weeks of October); Jultagi tightrope show with audience; Joseon wedding reenactment.
smartNotes.facilities: Everland: Smart Queue (free, in-app reservation system) is essential before 14:00 for T Express, Lost Valley, Safari World, Panda World — register the moment you enter the park. Folk Village: traditional street market food is the on-site option (Imsil cheese pizza, railroad hot dogs).
smartNotes.tip: Pick ONE primary destination — Everland or Folk Village — at booking; trying to do both leaves both feeling rushed. Pair Folk Village with Suwon Hwaseong Fortress (UNESCO, 15-20 min) for a coherent Joseon-themed half-day if you take Option B. For Everland, mid-week visits during peak season have materially shorter queues.

visitBasics.hours: Everland: 10:00-22:00 weekdays, 09:30-22:00 weekends/holidays (varies seasonally); Korean Folk Village: Mon-Thu 10:00-18:00, Fri-Sun 10:00-22:00
visitBasics.admission: Everland adult 1-day 54,000-64,000 KRW (20% foreigner discount at gate; Klook discounts often available); Korean Folk Village adult 37,000 KRW (Visit Korea official). Driver-guide assists with ticket purchase.
visitBasics.walking: High at Everland (5-10 km easily — large hilly park) — Korean Folk Village is high too (5-7 km on flat dirt/gravel paths)

highlights: Everland — T Express: Korea's first wooden roller coaster, 1.64 km / 46 m drop at 77° / 104 km/h, opened 2008
Everland — Zootopia + Panda World: ~2,000 animals incl. the Aibao-Fubao-Ruibao-Huibao panda family; Lost Valley amphibious-vehicle ecological safari
Korean Folk Village — 260+ Joseon-era houses on 991,735 m² (245 acres); first open-air museum in Korea

category: Double-Crater Volcanic Cone — 99 Peaks + Olle Trail 10 + WWII History

en_fields.description: **Songaksan Mountain (송악산)** is a **104 m double-crater parasitic volcanic cone** rising from Jeju's southwest tip in Daejeong-eup. The mountain is famous for its **'99 Peaks' (구십구봉)** folk count — a poetic naming of the dense scatter of small volcanic cones that radiate from the main mountain, formed by sequential eruption events that left **black + red volcanic ash deposits** still visible inside the crater rims. Geologically, Songaksan exhibits a **double-crater structure**: an **outer crater (~500 m diameter, ~1.7 km circumference)** and an **inner crater (~400 m diameter, ~69 m deep)** carved by a later subsidiary eruption inside the first. The visitor experience is the **2.8-km coastal loop trail** along the cliff edges, almost entirely **well-maintained wooden boardwalk + gentle stairs**, completing in **~75 minutes** at a moderate pace. The trail is the **central segment of Jeju Olle Trail Course 10 (Hwasun → Moseulpo)**. Panoramic views from the loop include — to the south — **Marado Island (Korea's southernmost point, 1.7 km offshore)** and **Gapado Island (flat barley-field island, 5.5 km offshore)**; to the southeast — **Hyeongje Islet ('Brother Islands') with their distinctive columnar-jointed twin rocks**; and to the northeast — **Sanbangsan Mountain (395 m tuff lava cone)** and **Hallasan (1,950 m, central island)**. **Historical layer**: the southern coastal cliffs hold **Imperial Japanese Army tunnels carved during WWII occupation** — kamikaze suicide-boat shelters and ammunition stores, similar to the 18 caves at Seongsan Ilchulbong. The tunnels are publicly accessible with interpretive signage. Wildlife: **free-roaming horses + ponies** graze the slopes (a Jeju visual signature dating to traditional pasture-land use). The **summit trail occasionally closes** for environmental conservation rotation; the coastal loop remains accessible year-round. **FREE admission**; small parking lot (often full at peak times). Strong sea winds even on sunny days — windbreaker recommended year-round.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: **Day's geological + historical climax** — and the catalog's only stop combining **double-crater volcanic cone geology + WWII colonial-history layer + 5-island panorama in a single 75-minute walk**. Songaksan sits **15 min west of Jusangjeolli** and **20 min east of Hyeopjae Beach** — the natural geographic pivot of the west-south route. Compared with **Sanbangsan Mountain (395 m, no summit trail) or Yongmeori Coast (tide-restricted access, 30-min walk)**, Songaksan is **104 m / 75-min loop / always-accessible coastal portion / WWII-layered**, making it the highest-yield southwest stop. The early-afternoon timing (13:40) catches **strong overhead light on the volcanic cliff strata** — distinct from typical sunset framing of the same stop, and saves Hyeopjae Beach for the actual sunset.

convenience.restroom: Available at parking area

smartNotes.facilities: Small café and food trucks at base in summer
smartNotes.tip: Strong wind exposure on summit — bring a windbreaker; horse riding available at base for extra fee

category: UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage Anchor — Jeju Diving Culture (2016)

en_fields.description: **Jeju Haenyeo Museum (제주 해녀 박물관)** is the institutional anchor for **'Culture of Jeju Haenyeo (women divers)'** — inscribed as **UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity in 2016**. Located in **Gujwa-eup**, the museum sits in the heart of Jeju's most haenyeo-active eastern coastal villages. The earliest written record of Jeju women divers dates to **1629**, in **'Description of Jeju's Natural Features' and 'Collected Works of Guchang' by Lee Geon (이건)**, where they were called **'Jamnyeo (潛女, diving women)'** — though oral tradition holds that the diving practice is far older, possibly **predating Joseon by centuries**. The museum documents the haenyeo's distinctive practice across **3 exhibition halls**: **(1) Daily Life + Equipment** — traditional diving gear (pre-rubber-suit cotton suits, *tewak* gourd floats, *bitchang* hand-spear, weighted nets), kitchen tools, and the **maternal lineage of dive-teaching** (mothers train daughters from age 6–7); **(2) Diving Practice + Categorization** — haenyeo are formally graded as **Hagun (junior, trainee), Junggun (mid-level), Sanggun (senior, who guides the others)**, with depth and breath-hold capacity defining rank; senior haenyeo dive to **10 m on a single 60-second breath-hold**, working **~7 hours per day, ~90 days per year**; **(3) Community + Future** — village cooperatives, ritual songs, the **diminishing population (under 4,000 active today, mostly 70+)**, and conservation efforts. Outdoor sculpture garden + replica fishing boat at the entrance courtyard. Admission: **Adult ₩1,100 / Youth ₩500 / FREE under 7 + over 65**. Hours: **09:00–18:00**, **closed Tuesdays and lunar new year/Chuseok**. Indoor ratio ~80% — strong rainy-day fallback anchor.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: **UNESCO Intangible heritage finale** — and the day's narrative climax. After the morning's natural + colonial-history layers (Hamdeok), the Joseon administrative heritage (Seongeup), the lava-tube UNESCO adjacency (Ilchulland), and the Triple UNESCO geological climax (Seongsan), the Haenyeo Museum **completes the day's UNESCO narrative arc** with UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage status (2016). Geographically this is **15-min coach from Seongsan**, the natural sequencing — guests who watched the 15:00 haenyeo demonstration at Seongsan now learn the full historical and cultural depth in museum context. **Indoor ratio ~80%** = the day's strongest rainy-day fallback anchor, also a welcome interior break after the Seongsan climb. Compared with **Jeju Folk Village Museum (themed reconstruction, ₩12,000)** or **Jeju National Museum (Jeju City center, off-route)**, the Haenyeo Museum is **₩1,100, on-route, and UNESCO-grade** — the highest yield for the day's UNESCO theme.

convenience.restroom: Multiple restrooms

smartNotes.photo: Outdoor sculpture park with haenyeo statues; traditional underwater life recreation
smartNotes.facilities: Café, gift shop, English exhibits
smartNotes.tip: Pair with a real haenyeo show (Seongsan or Udo) for full cultural experience

en_fields.description: The most scenically dramatic route, weaving together Jeju's southern volcanic geology, subtropical waterfalls, and traditional market food. Cheonjiyeon Falls in downtown Seogwipo is a 22 m single-tier waterfall with a 12 m wide × 20 m deep plunge pool, illuminated daily 09:00-22:00 and reached via a 1 km flat trail through subtropical forest where camphor trees (Natural Monument 163) grow. Daepo Jusangjeolli Cliff displays a 1 km coastal stretch of hexagonal basalt columns formed by cooling lava — part of the Jeju UNESCO Global Geopark. Sanbangsan Mountain is a 395 m trachytic lava dome formed 700,000-800,000 years ago, with the Sanbanggulsa cave temple at 145-150 m elevation reached by a stair climb; in spring the rapeseed flower fields at the base create the iconic golden-and-volcanic-grey contrast. Yakcheonsa Temple — at 122,100 m² considered the largest Buddhist temple complex in East Asia, with a 30 m-tall main hall, 5 m wooden Vairocana Buddha, and 18-ton bell — sits between Daepo and Jungmun. Jeongbang Falls is one of the few waterfalls in Asia that flows directly into the sea, while Seogwipo Olle Maeil Market closes the day with Hallabong tangerines, black-pork specialties, and Jeseong Bakery's octopus-stuffed buns.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: The strongest route for travelers who want geological variety in one day — a temple, a waterfall, a basalt cliff, a volcanic dome, and a traditional market all cover Jeju's signature 'volcanic island' identity. It also has the highest density of indoor-fallback options of the three routes (Yakcheonsa main hall, Olle Market covered arcade, Sanbanggulsa cave) — making it the strongest weather-flexible choice.

convenience.restroom: Available at all major stops; market has multiple
convenience.parking: Driver-guide handles all parking; Olle Market parking can be tight at weekends — private charter avoids this

smartNotes.photo: Cheonjiyeon's evening illumination is the unique shot (no other major Jeju waterfall illuminates nightly); Sanbangsan with spring rapeseed at the base is the springtime signature; Jusangjeolli's hexagons in late-afternoon side-light show the column geometry best.
smartNotes.facilities: Olle Market is the strongest single food stop on Jeju — Jeseong Bakery's octopus buns, mochi-pancakes, and Hallabong-juice stalls. The black-pork restaurants near Sanbangsan/Daejeong are also strong. Yakcheonsa's templestay vegetarian meal is bookable separately.
smartNotes.tip: For a rainy day on Jeju, this is the strongest of the three named routes — Yakcheonsa main hall, Sanbanggulsa cave, and Olle Market covered arcade together cover ~3-4 hours of indoor time. The Sanbangsan summit itself is closed; the cave temple at 150 m is the visitor endpoint.

visitBasics.hours: Most stops 09:00-17:30. Cheonjiyeon illuminated 09:00-22:00. Olle Market: 07:00-21:00 summer, 07:00-20:00 winter; night market 18:00-24:00 daily.
visitBasics.closed: Most stops open year-round; individual market shops close on rotating weekdays
visitBasics.admission: Cheonjiyeon 2,000 KRW; Jusangjeolli ~2,000 KRW; Sanbanggulsa ~1,000 KRW; Yakcheonsa free; Olle Market free entry. Driver-guide assists with tickets.
visitBasics.walking: Moderate — Cheonjiyeon trail flat 1 km, Sanbangsan stairs 0.6 mi to cave temple, market browse on flat covered arcade. Sanbangsan stairs are the bottleneck for low-mobility travelers.

highlights: Cheonjiyeon Falls: 22 m single-tier waterfall with daily night illumination, 1 km flat subtropical-forest trail; Mutae eel (Natural Monument 27) habitat; admission 2,000 KRW
Daepo Jusangjeolli Cliff: 1 km of hexagonal basalt columns up to 30-40 m tall, UNESCO Global Geopark; admission ~2,000 KRW
Sanbangsan + Yakcheonsa: 395 m trachytic lava dome + Asia's largest Buddhist temple complex; cave-temple climb to 150 m elevation; combined ~1,000-2,500 KRW
Olle Maeil Market: 620 m covered arcade, ~200 shops + ~140 vendors + 20-stall night market; signature Hallabong tangerines, black pork, octopus buns

poi_meta.note: Route option for private charter — represents a multi-POI bundle (Cheonjiyeon, Jusangjeolli, Sanbangsan, Yakcheonsa, Jeongbang, Olle Market); not a single matchable POI. All constituent POIs verified in KB v1.10.

category: Mythology + Volcanic Stone Culture — 300,000-pyeong outdoor museum

en_fields.description: **Jeju Stone Park (제주 돌문화공원, Jeju Dolmunhwa Gongwon)** is a **300,000-pyeong (~990,000 m²) open-air museum** in Jocheon, dedicated to Jeju's foundational creation myth: **Seolmundae Halmang (설문대 할망, the Giantess Goddess)** who, according to Jeju oral tradition, **created the island by scattering rocks from her apron** — Hallasan rose where she dropped the largest stone, and the **360+ oreum (parasitic cones) across the island are the smaller stones that fell as she walked**. The park's stone collection is **30+ years in the making**, curated from sites across Jeju to preserve the island's volcanic-rock heritage. Three walking courses lay out the experience: **Course 1 (~1 hour)** covers the headline museum + Sky Pond + Mother's Room; **all three courses (~2h 40min)** complete the full grounds including the Obaek Janggun (500 generals) Gallery, Stone Museum, traditional Jeju thatched-roof village, and Seolmundae Halmang Exhibition Hall. The site's signature contemporary architectural moment is **'Mother's Room (어머니의 방)'** — a cylindrical concrete chamber where a single overhead skylight beams shifting daylight onto a central stone sculpture, resolving the abstract giantess myth into a meditative architectural experience. Admission: **Adult ₩5,000 / Youth ₩3,500 / FREE under 13 + over 65**; **last Wednesday of every month is FREE Cultural Day**. Hours: **09:00–18:00 (last entry 17:00)**, **closed first Monday of each month**. Indoor ratio ~30% (museum + indoor halls); remainder outdoor walking — comfortable shoes essential.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: **Mythological + cultural opening anchor of the east loop**. Where the highlights-loop tour opens at Hallasan UNESCO geology, this nature-core route opens at Hallasan's **mythological origin story** — the same volcanic island, framed as oral-tradition creation rather than UNESCO geology. Setting Seolmundae Halmang as the day's first narrative beat means every subsequent stop (Seopjikoji's volcanic neck Seonbawi, Seongsan's tuff cone, Ilchulland's lava tube, Seongeup's stone-walled hanok) reads as downstream chapters of the giantess myth. **300,000 pyeong of outdoor walking** also front-loads the day's exposure — banking outdoor time before potential afternoon weather shifts. Compared with **Hallim Park (300,000 pyeong, ₩15,000, more diffuse botanical experience)**, Stone Park is **₩5,000, mythology-focused, and architecturally curated** — the highest-yield cultural opener in east Jeju.

convenience.restroom: At the entrance and inside the facilities

smartNotes.photo: Wider landscape compositions usually work better here than close-up portrait shots.
smartNotes.facilities: Among the early stops, this one generally has the strongest restroom and indoor facility setup.
smartNotes.tip: This stop works best as a calm, grounded opening rather than a rushed check-in-and-leave photo stop.

visitBasics.admission: KRW 5,000 adults / KRW 3,500 teens
visitBasics.walking: Easy to moderate

highlights: **300,000-pyeong outdoor museum** — Jeju's largest volcanic-stone heritage site
**Seolmundae Halmang myth** — Jeju's foundational creation legend, Hallasan + 360 oreum origin
**Course 1 (~1 hr)** vs full circuit (~2h 40min) — flexible time-budget walking
**Mother's Room (어머니의 방)** — cylindrical concrete chamber with skylight + stone sculpture
**Obaek Janggun (500 Generals) Gallery** — myth-inspired stone sculpture array
**30+ years** of curated volcanic-rock collection across Jeju
Reconstructed **traditional Jeju thatched-roof (jip) village** at the park's edge

category: Nami Island + Morning Calm + Petite France + Gangchon Railbike

en_fields.description: The most scenically diverse route, looping through Gapyeong's signature nature-and-culture cluster about 60-90 minutes northeast of Seoul. Nami Island is a 460,000 m² crescent-shaped river island reached by a 5-minute ferry, world-famous since the 2002 K-drama Winter Sonata for its tree-lined avenues (Metasequoia, Ginkgo, and Cherry). The Garden of Morning Calm is a 330,000 m² arboretum cultivating about 5,000 plant species — opened in 1996 by Han Sang-kyung of Sahmyook University and home to Korea's first natural-environment lighting festival (December 5 to March 15) where 30,000+ LEDs cover the garden after sunset. Petite France, opened in 2008, is a 16-building French-themed cultural village built around 'flowers, stars, and the Little Prince' — Korean drama fans will recognize it as the filming set for Beethoven Virus, Secret Garden, My Love from the Star, and most recently Bon Appétit Your Majesty (2025). Gangchon Rail Park / Bike adds a 30-40 minute pedal-rail ride along the disused Gyeongchun Line tracks beside the Bukhangang River. The route works year-round, with each season delivering a different signature: cherry blossoms (April), green tunnels (summer), foliage (October-early November), and snow + lighting festival (December-February).
en_fields.whyOnRoute: The strongest route for first-time Seoul visitors who want a nature-and-photo day with low walking demand and high Instagram return. All four stops are within 15-20 minutes of each other, the per-stop entrance fees are modest, and the seasonal signatures (cherry blossoms in April, foliage in October, snow + lighting festival in December-February) make repeat visits worthwhile.

convenience.restroom: Available at all stops

smartNotes.photo: Nami's Metasequoia avenue and Ginkgo lane are the iconic shots; Petite France's pastel houses against the green mountains; Morning Calm during the December-March lighting festival post-sunset is the absolute signature.
smartNotes.facilities: Nami Island has multiple cafés and a hanok-style restaurant on site; Petite France has snack stalls only (eat lunch elsewhere); Morning Calm's Food Garden serves Korean comfort food.
smartNotes.tip: If visiting in December-March, time the route to arrive at Morning Calm by 16:00 so you see the daytime garden first, then stay through illumination (~17:00 onward). Pre-book Rail Bike slots — they sell out on weekends.

visitBasics.hours: Nami Island: 07:30-21:00 daily; Garden of Morning Calm: 08:30-19:00 standard, 11:00-21:00/23:00 during winter lighting festival; Petite France: 09:00-18:00 (last entry 17:00); Rail Bike: 09:00-17:30 with timed slots
visitBasics.closed: All open year-round; Petite France closes some Mondays in off-season
visitBasics.admission: Nami 18,000 / Morning Calm 11,000 / Petite France 12,000 / Rail Bike ~30,000-35,000 per bike (1-4 people). Driver-guide assists with ticket purchase.
visitBasics.walking: Moderate — Nami Island walking loop is ~3 km flat; Morning Calm 1.5-2 hours of garden paths; Petite France gentle slopes between buildings

highlights: Nami Island: 460,000 m² river island, 5-min ferry, Winter Sonata Metasequoia avenue + Ginkgo lane; full-day pricing 18,000 KRW adult includes ferry
Garden of Morning Calm: 330,000 m² arboretum, 5,000 plant species, winter lighting festival Dec 5 - Mar 15 with 30,000+ LEDs; admission 11,000 KRW adult
Petite France: opened 2008, 16 French-style buildings, Saint-Exupéry / Little Prince theme, multi-K-drama filming site; admission 12,000 KRW adult, 19,500 combined with adjacent Italian Village
Gangchon / Gapyeong Rail Bike: 8 km pedal-rail along the old Gyeongchun Line, 30-40 min ride; ~30,000-35,000 KRW per 4-person bike

Gangchon Rail Bike is a single-use experience without a standalone KB entry.

en_fields.description: The most historically heavy route, pairing a half-day on Korea's Demilitarized Zone with an afternoon at Korea's largest premium-outlet mall. The DMZ portion typically covers Imjingak Peace Park (the Freedom Bridge and the rusting Bridge of Freedom), the 3rd Infiltration Tunnel (a 1,635 m North-Korean-built tunnel discovered 1978), Dora Observatory (high-zoom views into North Korea's Kaesong region), and Dorasan Station (the unused 'last station to North' on the would-be inter-Korean rail). Some sub-itineraries swap in the Paju Suspension Bridge or Camp Greaves history park. Paju Premium Outlets, opened in 2011 and at the time of opening Korea's largest premium outlet mall, hosts 230+ brands across two adjacent properties (Premium Outlets + Lotte Premium Outlets) with US/EU brand discounts of typically 25-50%. Heyri Art Valley — a 100,000 m² artist-village of galleries, cafés, and museums — is a frequent third stop if your group prefers art over shopping. Note: foreign passport holders must present passports at DMZ entry points; some sub-areas (Dorasan Station platform) are subject to military operational closures.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: The strongest route for travelers who want a substantive Korean War / inter-Korean-relations history experience without the rigid scheduling and bus-tour pacing of the standard JSA tour. The private charter format means the DMZ portion adapts to military operational closures (which are common) and the afternoon shifts to shopping or art depending on group interest. The Paju Outlets afternoon also balances the heavy history morning with practical retail — strong combination for travelers on a winter trip when outdoor walking time is limited.

convenience.restroom: Available at all DMZ stops and at Paju Outlets food courts
convenience.parking: Driver-guide handles all parking; DMZ has dedicated tour-bus lots

smartNotes.photo: Dora Observatory's high-zoom telescopes for the North Korea views; Freedom Bridge with its colorful prayer ribbons; the 1953 rusting locomotive at Imjingak — none photograph the inside of the 3rd Tunnel (cameras prohibited).
smartNotes.facilities: Lunch is best in Paju city (between DMZ and Outlets) — Paju is famous for grilled pork belly (samgyeopsal). DMZ-area food is limited and tourist-priced.
smartNotes.tip: Bring passports — required for DMZ entry. The 3rd Tunnel ramp is the bottleneck for low-mobility travelers — the monorail option (9,000 KRW return) covers the same descent without the steep walk. DMZ tours run on operational schedules so the driver-guide will confirm available stops the morning of the tour.

visitBasics.hours: DMZ stops 09:00-17:00 (last entry 15:30 most stops); Paju Premium Outlets 10:30-21:00 daily
visitBasics.closed: DMZ tour areas closed Mondays + when military operations require; Paju Outlets open year-round
visitBasics.admission: DMZ Combined Ticket ~12,000 KRW (3rd Tunnel + Dora Observatory + Dorasan Station); Paju Outlets free entry. Passport required at DMZ entry — driver-guide assists.
visitBasics.walking: Moderate — 3rd Tunnel descent is the most physically demanding section (steep 73 m ramp; monorail option ~9,000 KRW return); Paju Outlets are flat outdoor walking shopping center

highlights: 3rd Infiltration Tunnel: 1,635 m North-Korean-built tunnel discovered Oct 17, 1978; visitors descend ~73 m via shuttle monorail or steep walking ramp
Dora Observatory: high-zoom views into Kaesong (North Korea); on clear days the Kaesong Industrial Complex is visible
Imjingak Peace Park: Freedom Bridge (1953 prisoner-of-war exchange site) + Mangbaedan altar + 1953 rusting locomotive
Paju Premium Outlets: 230+ international brands at 25-50% off; Lotte Premium Outlets adjacent for another ~150 brands

poi_meta.note: Route option for private charter — DMZ-related POIs (Imjingak, 3rd Tunnel, Dora Observatory, Dorasan) will receive their dedicated KB entries in Batch 8 alongside the standalone DMZ tour (seoul-dmz-private-3rd-tunnel-suspension-bridge). Paju Premium Outlets does not get a standalone KB entry (single-use shopping stop). Facts above are verified via VisitKorea and DMZ tour operator pages.

category: Coastal Waterfall — Falls Directly Into Ocean (Asia Unique)

en_fields.description: **Jeongbang Falls (정방폭포, 'falls that drop straight to sea')** is the **only waterfall in Asia that falls directly into the ocean** — a **23 m vertical drop** straight off a basalt cliff into the Pacific, no intermediate pool, no riverbed mouth. The cascade is **8 m wide** with a 5 m base pool that reaches the open sea on every tide. This single geographic distinction is why Jeongbang is one of the **'Yeongjusipgyeong (영주십경, 10 Scenic Wonders of Jeju)'** — a Joseon-era literati canon of the island's most poetic landscapes. The Yeongjusipgyeong list, codified by **18th-century Confucian scholar Lee Won-jo (이원조)**, also includes Seongsan Sunrise, Hallasan summit, and Cheonjeyeon — meaning today's loop covers **3 of the 10** in a single day. Carved on the cliff face beside the falls is the inscription **'Seobulgwacha (서불과차, Seobul Headed Back West)'**, attributed to **Xu Fu (서복)**, the legendary Qin-dynasty alchemist sent by **Emperor Qin Shi Huang (~219 BCE)** to find the elixir of immortality. Local lore says he passed through here on his way home — the inscription is the etymological root of the place name **'Seogwipo' (서귀포, 'where Seo headed back')**. Access is via **130 stone steps** down to the cove (steepish — knee-friendly footwear recommended). Admission: **Adult ₩2,000 / Youth ₩1,000 / FREE under 7 and over 65**. The thunderous spray-cooled microclimate at the base is a noted summer relief spot. **Local haenyeo (해녀, women divers — UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage 2016)** sometimes sell fresh sea urchin, abalone, and conch directly at the cove on calm-sea days — a rare photographic opportunity to see the diving community at work outside their formal museum context.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: Jeongbang is the **only stop on the loop where geology, mythology, and place-name etymology converge in one frame** — the ocean-falling cascade explains 'Seogwipo' as literally as a place-name can be explained. Compared with **Cheonjeyeon (3-tier inland falls, ~3 km north)** the visit is **shorter, more dramatic, and walkable from lunch** — Cheonjeyeon requires a Jungmun detour and 30+ min on its forest loop. The 13:35 afternoon timing also catches the sun-on-falls window (14:00–14:30) for photography. From here the coach heads east on Route 1132 toward Seongsan — a 65-minute coastal drive showcasing the **eastern Jeju oreum field** through the windows.

convenience.restroom: Entrance area

smartNotes.photo: The rocky area below works well for atmosphere-rich shots, but footing and sea spray can make the experience feel more demanding.
smartNotes.facilities: Basic facilities are located near the entrance.
smartNotes.tip: Because of the stairs and rocky path, it usually feels more physically active on site than it appears on the map.

visitBasics.admission: KRW 2,000 adults / KRW 1,000 children
visitBasics.walking: Moderate, with stairs

highlights: **Asia's only waterfall falling directly into the ocean** — 23 m vertical drop, no riverbed
Falls are **8 m wide** with a 5 m base pool flushing into the Pacific each tide
One of the **'Yeongjusipgyeong'** — Joseon-era 10 Scenic Wonders of Jeju
**'Seobulgwacha' inscription** — attributed to Xu Fu (Qin alchemist, ~219 BCE), origin of place-name 'Seogwipo'
**130 stone steps** down to the cove — knee-friendly footwear recommended
Admission **₩2,000 adult / ₩1,000 youth / FREE under 7 + over 65**
Spray-cooled microclimate at the base — natural summer relief
Local **haenyeo (해녀, women divers)** sometimes sell fresh catches at the cove on calm days

name_en: West Route: Beaches, Tea & Cafe Coast

en_fields.description: The most relaxed and Instagram-friendly route, anchored on Jeju's western coastline of pale sand beaches, subtropical gardens, and tea fields. Hyeopjae Beach delivers the iconic Jeju shot — turquoise shallows, white shell-fragment sand, and Biyangdo Island floating offshore. Hallim Park (annexed to the beach area) is a 100,000 m² horticultural park with the Hyeopjae and Ssangyong lava caves inside its grounds and seasonal flower displays from camellia (winter) through tulips (spring) to lotus (summer). O'sulloc Tea Museum, opened in 2001 by AmorePacific on a working green-tea estate, draws design-minded visitors for its tea-plantation walks, the Innisfree Jeju House next door, and rotating tea-themed café menus including the famed green-tea soft-serve and roll cake. The day usually closes along Aewol Cafe Street, a 5 km coastal strip lined with sea-view cafés and restaurants where afternoon and sunset hours are the local rhythm. The driver-guide adapts the sequence depending on tide for the beaches and on weather for the tea fields.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: The strongest route for travelers who prefer relaxed pacing, photogenic backdrops, and curated café/dessert culture over UNESCO checklist completion. Walking volume is materially lower than the East route, the indoor-fallback density is good (Hallim Park caves + greenhouse, O'sulloc + Innisfree), and the route works equally well on a sunny or partly-cloudy day.

convenience.restroom: Available at all major stops; cafés have facilities for paying customers
convenience.parking: Driver-guide handles all parking; Aewol street parking is tight on weekends — private charter solves this

smartNotes.photo: Hyeopjae at low tide with Biyangdo behind is the iconic shot; O'sulloc tea-field rows photograph best in morning when light is soft; Aewol café terraces at golden hour close the day strongly.
smartNotes.facilities: Aewol has the strongest café density on Jeju — beat-the-crowd tip is to lock in your sunset café reservation in the morning so the slot is held. Hallim Park's interior garden cafe is a strong rainy-day option.
smartNotes.tip: If your itinerary needs a single weather-flexible Jeju day, this is the route — Hallim Park caves and the O'sulloc indoor museum cover ~3 hours of indoor time on a rainy day. Best paired with a sunset Aewol café finish; reserve cafés in the morning.

visitBasics.hours: Most stops 09:00-18:00; Aewol cafés typically 10:00-22:00 with last-order ~21:30
visitBasics.closed: Most stops open year-round; individual cafés close on rotating weekdays
visitBasics.admission: Hyeopjae Beach free; Hallim Park ~12,000 KRW adult; O'sulloc Tea Museum free; Aewol cafés pay-per-order. Driver-guide assists with park ticket purchase.
visitBasics.walking: Low-to-moderate — beach walks, flat park paths, café-to-café strolls; suitable for all ages and mobility levels

highlights: Hyeopjae Beach: white shell-fragment sand, turquoise shallows, Biyangdo island offshore — Jeju's signature beach shot; pairs with adjacent Geumneung Beach
Hallim Park: 100,000 m² subtropical park with Hyeopjae/Ssangyong lava caves on-site, plus Camellia / Tulip / Lotus seasonal festivals; admission ~12,000 KRW adult
O'sulloc Tea Museum: AmorePacific's flagship green-tea museum on a working tea estate; tea-tasting, plantation walking, and signature green-tea desserts; free admission
Aewol Cafe Street: 5 km of sea-view cafés (Bombom, Anthracite, Cafe del Mundo, Monsant) — best at golden hour

poi_meta.note: Route option for private charter — represents a multi-POI bundle (Hyeopjae, Hallim, O'sulloc, Aewol); not a single matchable POI. All constituent POIs verified in KB v1.10.

category: Themed gardens — Folk Village + Cactus + Bonsai + Sculpture Path

en_fields.description: **Ilchul Land's themed-garden complex** spreads across the same property as Micheon Cave, accessed via the park's signature **blue-line walking path** that connects all attractions. The **Folk Village** is a reconstructed traditional Jeju thatched-house compound where **live Jeju black pigs** (the same heritage breed designated Cultural Heritage Material No. 5) roam in a recreated village setting — visitors can see the **dolharubang stone grandfather statues**, **millstones**, **traditional kitchen + bedroom recreations**, and **'Eung Tae-seok' (응태석)** — the legendary 'stones that lay eggs', which folklore says will produce a girl-child if stroked rightward 3 times or a boy-child if stroked leftward 3 times. Beyond the village are the **Wild Flower Bonsai Garden**, **Cactus Greenhouse** (subtropical/desert plants under glass), **Subyeon Park** (with waterfall + reflecting pond + Chumsung Dae viewing pavilion), the **Sculpture Path** (rotating outdoor exhibition of Korean sculptors' works), and the **Aroma Footbath Cafe** (separate ₩5,000–8,000 fee, 30-min foot-spa session — popular after a long walking morning). Ilchul Land was a **filming location for Running Man Episode 89 (2012, Alice in Wonderland theme)** and the **K-drama 'What Star Do You Come From' (2006, MBC)**; signboards at filming spots help fans identify exact scene locations. The park also has a **Muslim prayer room** — relatively rare on Jeju and noted by Halal-traveling guests. Spring visit highlights the park's **camellia flowers, azaleas, and cherry blossoms** blooming alongside the walk.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: Ilchul Land's themed gardens are **the ideal pacing pivot** between the dim cave morning and the climactic Seongsan Ilchulbong sunset — a 75-min outdoor walking session through varied gardens lets guests reset. The Folk Village + black pigs also delivers the day's **only cultural-heritage stop** (Jeonnong-ro and Noksan-ro are nature-focused; Seongsan is geological). Selected over **Jeju Folk Village Museum** (much larger but requires a separate 2.5-hr visit) because Ilchul Land's compact format keeps the day's outdoor pacing.

convenience.restroom: Multiple

smartNotes.photo: Mix of natural and sculptural compositions works best in afternoon light.
smartNotes.tip: Combined ticket with Micheongul Cave gives ≈ 20% saving.

visitBasics.admission: Adults 12,000 KRW (combined with Micheongul)
visitBasics.walking: Easy (flat garden paths)

highlights: **Folk Village** with live Jeju black pigs (Cultural Heritage Material No. 5)
**'Eung Tae-seok' (응태석)** = legendary 'stones that lay eggs' — fertility folklore
**Wild Flower Bonsai Garden** + **Cactus Greenhouse** + **Subyeon Park waterfall**
**Sculpture Path** — rotating outdoor exhibition of Korean artists
**Aroma Footbath Cafe** (separate ₩5,000–8,000 fee)
**Running Man Episode 89 (2012, Alice in Wonderland)** + **'What Star Do You Come From' (2006 MBC)** filming

**Aroma Footbath Cafe** (Subyeon Park area) — a **rare on-site warm-water-immersion amenity** at a Jeju attraction (₩5,000–₩8,000 separate fee, towels provided) that lets cold-weather travelers (winter cherry-bloom shoulder season) recover thermal comfort mid-route — a feature not present at any other east-Jeju attraction

en_fields.description: Beyond the three recommended routes, the 10-hour charter window is fully customizable — mix stops across Gapyeong/DMZ/Yongin or send a fully custom plan in advance. Common custom additions include: Suwon Hwaseong Fortress (UNESCO World Heritage 1997 — 5.7 km of restored 18th-century walls in central Suwon), Pocheon Sanjeong Lake / Herb Island / Art Valley (1.5-hour drive northeast), Gwangmyeong Cave (former gold mine, now an LED-lit theme attraction), Starfield Library Suwon (architecturally striking 13 m bookshelves), Incheon Chinatown + Songdo Central Park, the K-pop entertainment-district tours of Hannam-dong / Gangnam, the Han River parks (Banpo Bridge rainbow fountain), and traditional markets like Gwangjang or Tongin. The driver-guide will sequence stops based on traffic patterns (avoiding Seoul rush hours of 07:00-09:00 and 17:00-20:00) and operating hours. Same-day-flight visits are well supported — the route can be back-loaded to drop at Incheon Airport at the end of 10 hours (state this at booking).
en_fields.whyOnRoute: The most flexible option of the four. Best for repeat Seoul visitors, travelers with niche interests (K-pop, K-drama, photography, food, family-with-kids, history-deep), and groups of 4-9 where the per-vehicle pricing vs. group-tour pricing tilts strongly toward the private charter. Same-day-flight scenarios are particularly well served — the 10 hours can include hotel checkout, sightseeing, and Incheon Airport drop-off in one continuous booking.

convenience.restroom: Available at all major stops

smartNotes.photo: Send 3-5 reference photos to the driver-guide in advance — it dramatically improves the quality of your final shot list
smartNotes.facilities: Lunch is your call — communicate dietary needs (halal, vegetarian, no-seafood, kids' menu) at booking so the guide can suggest appropriate restaurants
smartNotes.tip: For same-day-flight passengers: state your flight time at booking; the route will be sequenced to drop you at Incheon Airport ~3 hours before departure. Heyri Art Valley (Paju) and Suwon Hwaseong are the strongest single 'extra' stops most visitors don't plan for and most enjoy.

visitBasics.walking: Adjustable to your group's mobility

Pocheon day: Sanjeong Lake + Herb Island + Pocheon Art Valley — quieter than Gapyeong, granite-quarry photogenics
Incheon-Seoul half-day: Wolmido Park + Chinatown + Songdo Central Park — coastal alternative with airport-friendly close

poi_meta.note: Fully customizable route option — candidate POIs include both Batch 7 KB additions and POIs reserved for batches 8-9. Final stop list determined by passenger's request at booking.

en_fields.description: **Lunch at a guide-vetted southern Jeju restaurant** — typically in the Seogwipo or Andeok-myeon area, fitting the day's geographic flow (between tangerine farm and Camellia Hill). **Two typical options** depending on group preference and seasonal availability: **(1) Black Pork BBQ** (제주 흑돼지) — Jeju's signature ingredient; the indigenous **Jeju Black Pig was designated Natural Monument of Korea in 2015**, and pork-belly cuts are grilled over charcoal at the table with traditional banchan (winter banchan often includes Jeju winter cabbage kimchi + radish + Mom-guk seaweed soup); or **(2) Galchi-jorim** (갈치조림 — braised hairtail with radish, soy-chili sauce) — winter is the **Jeju hairtail peak season** (Nov–Feb), with the season's deepest fish from the southern coast reaching tables within hours of dockside landing. **Vegetarian options** available at most venues with advance notice (Bibimbap with Jeju seasonal vegetables + Mom-guk; the guide confirms ~30 min ahead). **Winter signature beverages**: hot **yujacha** (citron tea — same Jeju citrus family as Hallabong), **omegisul** (Seongeup family-recipe rice-millet liquor — winter warming spirit), and the local **Hallasan Soju** (~₩5,000). All venues in the rotation accept **major cards (Visa/Mastercard) + cash KRW**; bilingual photo menus (English/Chinese) are standard. **Lunch is NOT included** in standard pricing — typical out-of-pocket **₩15,000–25,000/pax**; inclusive lunch upgrade available on booking. **Winter rain backup**: all rotation venues have indoor seating, so a southern-Jeju winter shower never disrupts the meal.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: **Lunch positioning at 13:15 absorbs any morning Hallasan + tangerine schedule slip** — by this point in winter, the Eorimok trail and orchard schedules are behind the group, and lunch acts as flex buffer before the afternoon's three substantive stops. Geographic positioning between tangerine farm (Namwon-eup area) and Camellia Hill (Andeok-myeon) means **zero detour cost** — the lunch venue rotation is on the natural drive line.

name_en: Custom Route: Build Your Own Day

en_fields.description: Beyond the three recommended routes, the 9-hour charter window is fully customizable — you can mix stops across the East/West/South or send a fully custom plan in advance. Common additions include: Sangumburi Crater (Natural Monument 263 — a 650 m wide × 100 m deep phreatomagmatic crater famous for autumn silvergrass); Camellia Hill (already-bloomed camellia varieties Nov-Mar); Hueree Natural Park (alpaca + tangerine farm, family-favorite); ECO Land Theme Park (steam-locomotive ride through Gotjawal forest); Hamdeok Beach (turquoise shallows, family-friendly); Songaksan (coastal volcanic mountain with WWII tunnel network); Yongmeori Coast (cliff walk under Sanbangsan); Cafe the Cliff (Aewol sunset spot); the haenyeo Museum near Hado; and submarine/yacht tours from Seogwipo and Jungmun. Bring a full list to the driver-guide at pickup and they will sequence the day for traffic, ferry timing, and operating hours.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: The most flexible option of the four. Best for repeat Jeju visitors, travelers with niche interests (K-drama tracking, photography, food, family-with-kids), and groups of 4-9 where the per-vehicle pricing vs. group-tour pricing tilts strongly toward the private charter.

convenience.restroom: Available at all major stops

smartNotes.photo: Send 3-5 reference photos to the driver-guide in advance — it dramatically improves the quality of your final shot list
smartNotes.facilities: Lunch is your call — communicate dietary needs (halal, vegetarian, no-seafood, kids' menu) at booking so the guide can suggest appropriate restaurants
smartNotes.tip: The custom route is also the best fit for visitors with a flight that day — the 9-hour window can be back-loaded to drop you at Jeju Airport at the end. State this at booking so the route is sequenced toward the airport in the late afternoon.

Add a haenyeo (female-diver) seafood lunch — best near Hado (East) or Daejeong (West) where the haenyeo cooperatives are active
Combine a beach morning with a mountain or temple afternoon (Hyeopjae + Sanbangsan, or Hamdeok + Sangumburi)
Pair with K-drama filming locations: Seopjikoji ('All In'), Hyeopjae area ('Boys Over Flowers'), Cheonjiyeon ('Big')

poi_meta.note: Fully customizable route option — candidate POIs above are verified in KB v1.10 but not prescribed; final stop list determined by passenger's request at booking.

en_fields.description: Lunch is YOUR choice and timed flexibly around the day's pace — your driver-guide offers three vetted options matched to your group's preferences: **(1) Gwangjang Market food alley** — the working-class streetfood experience opened 1905 (Korea's first permanent traditional market), made world-famous by Anthony Bourdain's 'Parts Unknown.' Bindaetteok mung-bean pancakes (5,000-7,000 KRW), mayak gimbap, yukhoe raw beef tartare. CASH-ONLY at most stalls. **(2) Insadong traditional restaurant** — sit-down Korean cuisine in a hanok setting; hansik prix-fixe sets featuring banchan (side dishes), bibimbap, kimchi-jjigae stew. Mid-tier 15,000-25,000 KRW per person. **(3) Myeongdong Kyoja or Hadongkwan** — Korean institutions: Myeongdong Kyoja for handmade kalguksu (knife-cut noodles) and mandu since 1966; Hadongkwan for Seolleongtang (oxbone soup) since the 1930s. Mid-tier 12,000-20,000 KRW. **(4) Royal court cuisine at Korea House** — premium hanjeongsik (table d'hôte royal cuisine) in a hanok complex (recognized with three ribbons in the 2025 Blue Ribbon Survey); 60,000-120,000 KRW per person, requires 60-min commitment. Communicate dietary needs (vegetarian, halal, no spicy) at booking — driver-guide adjusts accordingly.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: Genuine lunch customization is the private-tour value driver — group bus tours commit to a single lunch venue, but private cruise passengers often have specific dietary needs or strong preferences (e.g., 'no streetfood' for some / 'authentic only' for others). The 4-option menu covers 95%+ of mainstream cruise-passenger preferences without forcing a single choice.

convenience.restroom: Restaurant restrooms

smartNotes.tip: Communicate dietary preferences (vegetarian, halal, no-spicy, gluten-free) at booking. Royal court cuisine at Korea House requires advance reservation — book 24+ hr ahead. Cash recommended for Gwangjang Market option (10,000-20,000 KRW per person).

visitBasics.hours: Most options 11:00-15:00 lunch service
visitBasics.admission: Pay-as-you-go (12,000 — 120,000 KRW range depending on choice)
visitBasics.walking: Easy — venue-dependent

en_fields.description: **Return drive to original pickup hotels** in Jeju City and Jungmun-area accommodations. The route from Cheonjeyeon Falls (Jungmun) splits — **Jungmun-area hotels are dropped off first** (~5 min from the falls), then the minicoach continues to Jeju City via Highway 1135 (~50 min). Drop-off is **typically at the original pickup hotel lobby**; guests who joined at off-hotel meeting points are returned to the same point unless otherwise requested. **Winter dispatch consideration**: if Route 1100 (the Hallasan ascent road used in the morning) has accumulated snow or freezing rain by evening, the return uses **alternative Route 1135 + 16** to avoid altitude exposure on the ride home. **Vehicle-side amenities**: hot tea/coffee remains available in the in-vehicle cooler; the guide can answer follow-up questions about Jeju (geology, citrus history, Korean dating-show filming, food tradition) during the return drive — a small-group day-tour's natural conversational close. If guests have **next-day Jeju International Airport flights**, the guide can advise on **Bus 600** from Jungmun-area hotels (~75 min to airport) or downtown Jeju City taxi/Bus 100 connections (~30 min). Tip-jar protocol: guides do not solicit gratuity, but a customary tip range of **₩10,000–20,000/pax** is appreciated for full-day winter tours (cash or KakaoPay).
en_fields.whyOnRoute: **Drop-off timing 17:30 → 18:30 arrival** matches **post-sunset winter dinner window** in both Jeju City and Jungmun — guests have evening hours intact for hotel dinner or further exploration. Splitting Jungmun (drop first) vs Jeju City (drop second) optimizes total drive time across the 8–15 pax mix where guests typically split ~30/70 between Jungmun and Jeju City accommodations. Winter route flexibility (1100 vs 1135+16) protects against late-day road conditions that morning route doesn't yet reflect.
highlights: **Jungmun hotels dropped first** (~5 min from Cheonjeyeon), then Jeju City (~50 min)
Return at original pickup hotel lobby or off-hotel meeting point
**Winter alternative routing** (Route 1135 + 16) when Route 1100 has overnight ice
Guide-led conversational close — Jeju Q&A during return drive
**Bus 600** advised for next-day airport connections from Jungmun
Customary tip range ₩10,000–20,000/pax (not solicited; cash or KakaoPay)

Jeju Provincial Government (Highway 1135 + Route 16 winter alternates)

en_fields.description: **Hotel pickup window 08:30–09:00** from designated Jeju City or Jungmun-area hotels. Driver and English-speaking guide arrive in a **minicoach (8–15 pax shared)** with name placard at the hotel lobby; the operator's 24/7 contact number is provided at booking. Pickup catchment includes downtown Jeju City hotels (Maison Glad, Lotte City, Ramada Plaza, etc.), Jeju Airport-area accommodations, and the Jungmun Tourist Complex (Lotte Hotel Jeju, Shilla Jeju, Hyatt). Guests outside the standard pickup zone can join at one of the operator's three meeting points (Jeju Bus Terminal, Halla Department Store, Yeon-dong Crossroads). **Winter dispatch consideration**: morning departure is delayed by ~15 min when overnight snowfall on Route 1100 (the Hallasan ascent road) is reported — the guide confirms road status with the operator's morning weather brief and adjusts pickup accordingly. Guests are advised to dress in **winter layers + waterproof outer shell** + non-slip footwear (the day's exposure includes Hallasan snow trail, open-air Camellia Hill garden, outdoor tangerine orchard, and Jusangjeolli's wind-exposed cliff edge). Crampons can be rented at the Hallasan trailhead (~₩8,000) but the operator carries 2–3 spare sets in the vehicle. All beverages, snacks, and lunch are out-of-pocket; the vehicle has a small cooler with hot tea/coffee available throughout the day.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: **Pickup is timed to clear Hallasan summit access window** — Eorimok last-entry is **~11:30 in winter** (vs ~12:30 in other seasons), so the day must start by 09:00 to give the group full hike time before the entry cutoff. Layered hotel pickup (downtown + Jungmun + 3 meeting points) absorbs guests across Jeju's two main accommodation clusters in a single sweep, keeping the minicoach at the 8–15 pax target without dispatching a separate van.
highlights: **Pickup window 08:30–09:00** from Jeju City + Jungmun hotels
Minicoach (8–15 pax shared); English-speaking guide + driver
Three off-hotel meeting points: Bus Terminal, Halla Dept Store, Yeon-dong Crossroads

en_fields.description: **60-minute reset in central Seogwipo (서귀포)** — Jeju's southern city, famous for fresh seafood landed at **Seogwipo Maeil Olle Market (서귀포매일올레시장)** and the abalone-rich coast. Default rotation depending on season and group size: **galchi-jorim (갈치조림, braised cutlassfish stew)** at a Seogwipo specialist when boats are landing in season (Sep–Feb), **jeonbok-bibimbap (전복비빔밥, abalone over rice)** year-round at a market-facing eatery, or **heuk-doeji gui (흑돼지구이, Jeju black pig BBQ)** for guests preferring grilled meat. Black pig (***Sus scrofa* domesticus, Jeju black variety**) was designated **Cultural Heritage Material No. 5 (2015)** and remains a Jeju protected breed. Vegetarian alternative on request: **bibim-guksu (비빔국수, cold spicy noodles)** with seasonal vegetables. The lunch spot is **a 5-minute walk from Jeongbang Falls** — guests with appetite for a quick post-lunch coffee can stop at the market's tea/cafe row before continuing on foot.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: Seogwipo central placement makes **Jeongbang Falls the next stop on foot** (no coach transit between lunch and stop 4) — recovers ~15 minutes vs. a Jungmun-area lunch. Galchi + black pig + abalone are Jeju's three signature proteins, all locally landed/raised — guests get the food-identity anchor of the day in one sitting. Pace also breaks the morning's Hallasan + cliff walking before the afternoon Seongsan climb.

en_fields.description: **60-minute reset between Jusangjeolli and Cheonjeyeon Falls** — Jungmun resort district is just 3 km from both stops, with a dense restaurant strip catering to both tourist and local clienteles. Default rotation by season and group: **galchi-jorim (갈치조림, braised cutlassfish)** at a Seogwipo-side specialist when boats are landing (Sep–Feb), **jeonbok-bibimbap (전복비빔밥, abalone over rice)** year-round, or **heuk-doeji (흑돼지, Jeju black pig BBQ)** for guests preferring grilled meat. Jeju black pig (***Sus scrofa* domesticus, Jeju variety**) is **Cultural Heritage Material No. 5 (2015)** — a protected breed traceable to ~12th century records. Vegetarian alternative on request: **bibim-guksu (비빔국수)** with seasonal Jeju vegetables. **Halal-friendly options** (ingredient-disclosed) available at one venue in the rotation — request at pickup. After lunch the coach is ~5-min drive to Cheonjeyeon entrance — minimal post-meal transit.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: Jungmun positioning means **5-minute hops to both Jusangjeolli (before) and Cheonjeyeon (after)** — recovers ~15 min vs. a Seogwipo-central lunch. Galchi + black pig + abalone are Jeju's three signature proteins, all locally sourced — guests get the food-identity anchor of the day in one sitting before the afternoon's tea + cafe sequence, which is light on solid food.

category: Midday Reset — East Jeju Local Cuisine

en_fields.description: **60-minute reset on the east coast** between Seopjikoji and Seongsan Ilchulbong — Seongsan-eup is one of Jeju's premier seafood landing zones, with daily catches supplying the harbor-side restaurant strip. Default rotation: **mulhoe (물회, cold raw-fish soup)** in summer (June–September), a Jeju summer staple where seasonal white-fleshed fish (yellowtail, sea bass) is served in chilled vinegared broth; **galchi-jorim (갈치조림, braised cutlassfish)** in winter (October–February); or **heuk-doeji gui (흑돼지구이, Jeju black pig BBQ)** year-round, the heritage breed designated **Cultural Heritage Material No. 5 (2015)**. **Vegetarian alternative**: **bibim-guksu (비빔국수)** with seasonal Jeju vegetables. **Halal-friendly options** available at one venue in the rotation — disclose at pickup. The lunch venue sits **10-min coach from Seongsan Ilchulbong entrance**, so guests preserve full afternoon energy for the cone climb.
en_fields.whyOnRoute: East-coast positioning means **10-min hop to Seongsan Ilchulbong** afterward — preserves cone-climb energy. Mulhoe is also an east-Jeju regional specialty (less common in Jeju City), so the geographic placement adds culinary identity to the lunch slot. Pace also breaks the morning's Stone Park + Seopjikoji walking before the afternoon's Seongsan ascent + cave exploration.
